You are a relationship management expert. Write referral acknowledgment messages that make partners want to send more.

Create thank-you messages for 4 stages of a referred client's journey:

**1. When I receive the referral** (within 2 hours):
Write 3 messages — email, text, and phone call script. Under 40 words each. Reference the referral by name. Commit to keeping the partner informed.

**2. After the first meeting with the referred client:**
Write a brief update message (under 50 words) that lets the partner know I met with their referral and things are moving forward — without violating client confidentiality.

**3. When the deal closes:**
Write a thank-you message (under 75 words) that includes specific results and reinforces the partnership. Pair with a gift recommendation — 3 options at different price points ($25, $50, $100+) that are memorable, not generic.

**4. Annual summary to partners:**
Write a year-end message (under 100 words) summarizing the referrals received, outcomes, and expressing genuine gratitude. This should feel personal, not corporate.

Example of a GOOD immediate thank-you:
"Hey Sarah — just got off the phone with the Johnsons. Great people. I can see why you work with them. I'll take excellent care of them and keep you posted. Thank you for thinking of me."

Example of a BAD immediate thank-you:
"Thank you for the referral. I will provide them with the highest level of service."

Do NOT be generic — reference the specific referral and partner by name in every template.
Do NOT send a thank-you only after closing — acknowledge immediately.
Do NOT forget to close the loop — partners who never hear what happened stop referring.
quickStart: Write a text message I send within 2 hours of receiving a referral from a partner. Under 30 words. Specific and warm.

title: Reverse Referral Strategy — Give First
bestFor: ** Agents who want referrals but haven't sent any
prompt:
You are a reciprocity strategist. Help me build referral partnerships by sending referrals FIRST — before I ever ask for one in return.

I currently work with clients who could use: [LIST SERVICES YOUR CLIENTS TYPICALLY NEED — mortgage, insurance, legal, accounting, home inspection, moving, etc.].

Create my "give-first" referral plan:

1. **10 referral opportunities I'm probably missing** — situations in my existing client work where I SHOULD be referring to partners but don't (because I haven't thought about it or don't have a partner yet). Describe each situation and the partner type who benefits.

2. **The referral introduction script** for each of my top 3 partner types — what I say to my client when recommending a partner. Under 40 words each. Should feel like a friend's recommendation, not a corporate partnership.

Example: "The Hendersons are going to need a great insurance agent for their new place. [To client:] 'I've got someone you'll love — Sarah Chen at Allstate. She handles most of my buyers and she's ridiculously responsive. Want me to connect you?'"

3. **The "heads-up" message to my partner** when I'm sending them a referral — under 30 words. Gives them context so they're prepared.

4. **The tracking system** — a simple format for tracking referrals I SEND:
| Date | Referred Client | Referred To | Service Needed | Outcome | Acknowledged? |

Do NOT keep score publicly — the tracking is for my awareness, not for guilting partners.
Do NOT refer to bad professionals just to build a partnership — quality matters.
Do NOT expect immediate reciprocation — the timeline is months, not days.
quickStart: Give me 5 situations in a typical real estate transaction where I should be referring my client to a professional partner (and building a referral relationship in the process).

title: Agent-to-Agent Referral Network Building
bestFor: ** Agents who want referral income from out-of-area moves
prompt:
You are a real estate referral network expert. Help me build relationships with agents in other markets for mutual referral exchange.

I'm based in [YOUR MARKET]. My clients most commonly relocate to/from: [LIST MARKETS — or "I don't know yet"]. I specialize in [NICHE].

Create my agent referral strategy:

1. **How to identify the right agents in other markets** — 5 criteria for choosing agents to partner with (not just "they're in that market" — what makes a good referral partner agent). Include where to find them (specific platforms, groups, conferences).

2. **The first outreach message** to an agent in another market — under 75 words. Proposes a reciprocal relationship without being presumptuous.

3. **The "get to know you" virtual coffee agenda** — 5 questions to ask in a 15-minute video call that help me decide if this agent is someone I'd trust with my clients.

4. **The referral exchange agreement** (informal) — a simple framework covering:
   - Referral fee structure (standard percentages)
   - Communication expectations (updates, timelines)
   - What happens if the referral doesn't work out
   - How often to check in even when there are no active referrals

5. **Maintaining the relationship** — 3 low-effort monthly touchpoints to keep agent-to-agent referral relationships warm.

Do NOT suggest I join every agent Facebook group — quality over quantity.
Do NOT ignore the trust factor — help me vet agents I'd be sending my clients to.
Do NOT make the referral fee the centerpiece — relationships come first.
quickStart: Write a 3-sentence message to an agent in another market proposing a referral exchange relationship. Professional but human.

title: Design a Monthly Power Partner Breakfast
bestFor: ** Agents ready to create a structured referral group
prompt:
You are an event design consultant for business networking. Help me create a monthly breakfast meeting for my top referral partners.

I want to invite [NUMBER] professionals from different industries who share clients with me. The goal: a recurring meeting where we exchange leads, market intelligence, and strengthen partnerships.

Design the complete event:

1. **The ideal member composition** — which 6-8 professional types to invite (only 1 per industry to avoid competition). List them and explain why each.

2. **The invitation message** (under 100 words) — for the very first meeting. Should communicate: what this is, what's in it for them, the time commitment, and the vibe (professional but casual).

3. **The 45-minute meeting agenda:**
   - [5 min] Opening/check-in structure
   - [10 min] Lead sharing round (format for each person to share needs)
   - [10 min] Market intelligence exchange
   - [10 min] Spotlight: one member goes deep on their business
   - [5 min] Commitments and close
   - [5 min] Buffer/social time

4. **The rules of engagement** — 5 ground rules that keep the group productive (attendance expectations, confidentiality, reciprocity norms).

5. **Logistics:** recommended venue type, day/time, who pays, and how to handle members who stop showing up.

Do NOT make this a sales pitch meeting — it's a value exchange.
Do NOT invite more than 8 people — the group loses intimacy above that.
Do NOT skip the "spotlight" — it's what makes members feel valued and keeps them coming back.
quickStart: Give me a 5-step agenda for a 45-minute monthly breakfast meeting with 6-8 referral partners from different industries. Include a lead-sharing format.

title: Joint Client Education Events with Partners
bestFor: ** Agents who want to create shared value with referral partners
prompt:
You are an event marketing strategist. Help me design a client education event I co-host with 2-3 referral partners that strengthens all of our businesses.

My market: [YOUR MARKET]. My partners for this event: [LIST — e.g., "mortgage lender, home inspector, real estate attorney"]. Our shared audience: [DESCRIBE — e.g., "first-time homebuyers," "investors," "downsizers"].

Design:

1. **Event concept and title** — 3 options for event names that attract attendees without sounding salesy. Under 10 words each.

2. **60-minute event agenda** with each partner's speaking segment:
   - Time allocation per speaker
   - Topic for each partner (specific to their expertise)
   - Q&A structure
   - How to handle the "sales" question — what IS and ISN'T appropriate to promote

3. **The invitation copy** (under 100 words) — for email. Should emphasize what attendees LEARN, not who's presenting.

4. **Lead-sharing agreement** — how to split contacts, follow up responsibilities, and credit rules. Keep it simple: 5 bullet points.

5. **Post-event follow-up plan** — who follows up with which attendees and when. Include a sample follow-up message (under 50 words) each partner sends.

Do NOT make this a panel of sales pitches — each segment must deliver genuine education.
Do NOT let any partner dominate the time — equal airtime matters.
Do NOT skip the lead-sharing conversation before the event — have it upfront.
quickStart: Give me a title and 4-segment agenda for a "First-Time Homebuyer Workshop" co-hosted by a real estate agent, mortgage lender, and home inspector. 60 minutes.

title: Annual Partner Appreciation Strategy
bestFor: ** Agents who want to retain and deepen existing referral relationships
prompt:
You are a relationship retention specialist. Help me design an annual appreciation plan for my top referral partners — people who sent me business this year.

I have approximately [NUMBER] active referral partners. My budget for partner appreciation is [$AMOUNT] for the year.

Create:

1. **A tiered appreciation plan:**

| Tier | Criteria | Number of Partners | Annual Appreciation Budget Per Person | Actions |
|------|----------|-------------------|--------------------------------------|---------|
| Platinum | 5+ referrals/year | [NUMBER] | | |
| Gold | 2-4 referrals/year | [NUMBER] | | |
| Silver | 1 referral/year | [NUMBER] | | |

For each tier, list 3 specific appreciation actions (not just "send a gift" — what gift, what gesture, what experience).

2. **5 appreciation gestures that cost under $50 but feel like $200** — creative ideas that show thoughtfulness over budget. Describe each in 2-3 sentences.

3. **The year-end thank-you message** (under 100 words) — personalized, references specific referrals and outcomes. Write one complete example.

4. **The annual partnership review meeting** — a simple agenda for a 20-minute year-end check-in:
   - Review the year's referrals (both directions)
   - What worked / what to improve
   - Goals for next year
   - Personal check-in (how are they doing?)

Do NOT send generic gift baskets — those say "my assistant ordered this."
Do NOT skip the partners who sent just 1 referral — they're your growth opportunity.
Do NOT make appreciation feel transactional — "Thanks for the referrals, keep them coming!" is gross.
quickStart: Give me 3 ways to thank a referral partner who sent me business this year that cost under $50 but feel genuinely personal.

title: LinkedIn Profile Optimization for Referral Partners (Not Clients)
bestFor: ** Agents whose LinkedIn reads like a client-facing advertisement
prompt:
You are a LinkedIn strategist. Optimize my profile to attract referral PARTNERS — lenders, attorneys, financial advisors — not just buyers and sellers.

My current headline: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE]. My current About section: [PASTE OR DESCRIBE — or "empty"]. My market: [MARKET]. My specialty: [NICHE]. My referral partnership style: [DESCRIBE — e.g., "I'm highly communicative," "I always close the loop," "I send referrals before I ask for them"].

Rewrite:

1. **3 headline options** (under 120 characters each) that position me as a great professional to partner with — not just an agent looking for clients.

2. **About section** (under 1,500 characters) structured as:
   - Hook that speaks to professionals, not consumers (first 2 lines visible before "see more")
   - What I bring to partnerships
   - My approach to client care (what matters to someone referring THEIR client to me)
   - A personal line that makes me human
   - How to connect with me

Example of a GOOD headline:
"Real Estate | I send my partners warm referrals and close the loop every time | [Market]"

Example of a BAD headline:
"Helping Buyers & Sellers Achieve Their Real Estate Dreams | Top Producer"

Do NOT use "real estate dreams," "journey," or "passion" anywhere.
Do NOT list awards or rankings in the headline — save those for the experience section.
Do NOT write in third person ("John is a dedicated agent...").
quickStart: Rewrite my LinkedIn headline to attract professional referral partners, not just clients. Current headline: [YOUR HEADLINE]. I want lenders and attorneys to think "I should connect with this person."

title: LinkedIn Connection Request Messages for Referral Targets
bestFor: ** Agents sending blank connection requests or generic ones
prompt:
You are a LinkedIn outreach specialist. Write personalized connection request messages for reaching out to potential referral partners on LinkedIn.

Write connection request messages (under 300 characters — LinkedIn's limit) for 8 professional types:

1. Mortgage lender in my market
2. Divorce attorney
3. Estate/probate attorney
4. Financial advisor
5. CPA
6. Insurance agent
7. HR/relocation manager at a local company
8. Agent in another market (for referral exchange)

Each message must:
- Reference something specific about them (include a [PLACEHOLDER] for their name, company, or recent post)
- Explain WHY I'm connecting in one clause
- NOT pitch or ask for anything
- Sound like a real person, not a bot

Example of a GOOD request:
"Hi Sarah — saw your post about first-time buyer challenges. I work with a lot of first-time buyers on the agent side and would love to connect. — [My Name]"

Example of a BAD request:
"Hi, I'd like to add you to my professional network on LinkedIn."

Then write 3 "post-acceptance" follow-up messages (under 100 words each) — what I send AFTER they accept. These start the relationship without pitching.

Do NOT copy-paste the same message to all 8 types.
Do NOT include the word "synergy" or "mutually beneficial."
Do NOT send a pitch as the follow-up to an accepted connection — start a conversation.
quickStart: Write a LinkedIn connection request message (under 300 characters) to a mortgage lender in my market. Reference their work, not mine. Warm and human.

title: LinkedIn Content Strategy for Professional Positioning
bestFor: ** Agents who post for clients but nothing that attracts professional partners
prompt:
You are a LinkedIn content strategist for B2B professional networking. Create a content plan that attracts referral partners, not consumers.

I am a real estate agent in [MARKET] who wants lenders, attorneys, financial advisors, and other professionals to see my content and think "I want to work with this person."

Create:

1. **10 LinkedIn post ideas** that appeal to PROFESSIONAL audiences — not buyers/sellers. For each, provide:
   - The topic (1 sentence)
   - Why it attracts partners (1 sentence)
   - The format (text post, carousel, poll, article)

2. **3 complete LinkedIn posts** (under 200 words each) ready to publish:
   - Post 1: A market insight that partners can share with THEIR clients
   - Post 2: A "what I look for in a great [lender/attorney/advisor]" post (that flatters your best partners without naming them)
   - Post 3: A partnership success story (how a great referral relationship benefited a shared client)

3. **The engagement strategy** — a daily 10-minute routine:
   - Whose posts to engage with (categories, not names)
   - What kind of comments build relationships (with 3 examples of strong comments vs. weak ones)

Do NOT post the same client-facing content you put on Instagram — LinkedIn is a different audience.
Do NOT write "just listed/just sold" posts — those are for clients, not partners.
Do NOT create content that positions you as desperate for referrals — position yourself as valuable to partner WITH.
quickStart: Write 1 LinkedIn post (under 150 words) that would make a mortgage lender or attorney want to partner with me. Topic: something about the current market that's relevant to their clients too.

title: Instagram DM Strategy for Professional Networking
bestFor: ** Agents who want to connect with local professionals on Instagram
prompt:
You are a social media networking strategist. Help me use Instagram DMs to initiate professional relationships with local business owners and allied professionals — not to pitch, but to connect.

My market: [YOUR MARKET]. Professionals I want to connect with on Instagram: [LIST — e.g., "local mortgage lenders, financial advisors, popular restaurant owners, fitness studio owners"].

Create:

1. **How to find and identify the right professionals** on Instagram — 5 search and discovery tactics specific to finding local professionals (hashtags, location tags, "suggested follows," etc.).

2. **The engagement-before-DM strategy** — what to do for 1-2 weeks BEFORE sending a DM:
   - How many posts to like/comment on
   - What kind of comments to leave (3 examples of good comments on a professional's post — not "Great post!")
   - How to get on their radar before reaching out

3. **5 Instagram DM templates** for different professional types — each under 40 words. These are first messages. They must feel casual and Instagram-appropriate (not LinkedIn-formal).

Example of a GOOD Instagram DM:
"Hey! I've been following your content for a while — your tips on [TOPIC] are really solid. I'm a local agent and I think our clients overlap. Would love to connect sometime."

Example of a BAD Instagram DM:
"Hi [NAME], I'm a real estate agent and I'd love to discuss a mutually beneficial referral partnership."

Do NOT send a DM without at least 2 weeks of engagement on their content first.
Do NOT write DMs that read like emails — match the platform's casual tone.
Do NOT follow up aggressively if they don't respond — one follow-up max, after 2 weeks.
quickStart: Write a casual Instagram DM (under 30 words) from a real estate agent to a local mortgage lender proposing a casual connection. Instagram-appropriate tone, not formal.

title: Online Community Participation Strategy
bestFor: ** Agents who want to build authority in Facebook Groups, Reddit, Nextdoor
prompt:
You are a community networking strategist. Help me build professional reputation and referral opportunities through strategic participation in online communities.

I am an agent in [YOUR MARKET]. I want to participate in online communities to build expertise recognition and generate referral relationships.

Create strategies for 3 platforms:

**1. Local Facebook Groups:**
- 3 types of groups to join (with examples of group name formats to search for)
- The engagement rules: how often to post, what to post, what NEVER to post
- 5 example responses to common questions in local groups that demonstrate expertise without being promotional (write them out — each under 75 words)
- How to transition from "helpful group member" to "the person everyone recommends"

**2. Nextdoor:**
- The profile optimization (what to include, what to skip)
- 3 types of posts that build credibility
- The response strategy when someone asks "Does anyone know a good realtor?"
- How NOT to respond (the slimy version vs. the credible version)

**3. Reddit (local subreddit + r/realestate):**
- The long game: why Reddit rewards genuine helpfulness and punishes promotion
- 5 types of comments that build authority
- What NEVER to do on Reddit as a professional

Do NOT be promotional in any example — online communities punish overt self-promotion.
Do NOT ignore community rules — include a note about reading group rules before posting.
Do NOT treat online communities as lead gen channels — treat them as reputation-building channels.
quickStart: Write a response (under 75 words) to someone in a local Facebook group asking "Is now a good time to buy?" that demonstrates expertise without being self-promotional. I'm an agent in [MARKET].

title: Converting Online Connections to Real-World Relationships
bestFor: ** Agents with lots of online connections but few real relationships
prompt:
You are a digital-to-physical relationship strategist. Help me convert LinkedIn connections and social media contacts into real-world professional relationships.

I have approximately [NUMBER] LinkedIn connections and [NUMBER] Instagram followers among local professionals. Very few of these are people I've actually met in person.

Create:

1. **The "coffee meeting" invitation playbook** — 5 different messages (under 75 words each) for inviting an online connection to meet in person. Each for a different scenario:
   - We've been engaging on LinkedIn for months but never met
   - They liked/commented on several of my posts
   - We have a mutual connection
   - I attended their webinar or event virtually
   - We're in the same online group/community

2. **The "joint event" strategy** — how to invite an online connection to attend a networking event, conference, or community event together. Write the invitation (under 60 words).

3. **The pacing guide** — a timeline showing the right cadence:
   - Week 1-2: Engage with their content
   - Week 3-4: DM or comment with a personal note
   - Week 5-6: Suggest a call or coffee
   - Week 7+: Meet in person
   Show what happens at each stage.

4. **The "collaboration proposal"** — 3 ways to propose working together with an online connection (joint webinar, co-authored post, shared community event). Write a message for each (under 60 words).

Do NOT rush the timeline — moving too fast from online to in-person feels aggressive.
Do NOT suggest meeting for "business" — suggest meeting to connect.
Do NOT propose a meeting without first establishing digital rapport (minimum 2-3 meaningful interactions).
quickStart: Write a LinkedIn message (under 50 words) inviting a professional connection I've been engaging with online to grab coffee in person. Casual, no pressure.

title: LinkedIn Engagement Routine — The 10-Minute Daily Playbook
bestFor: ** Agents who know LinkedIn matters but don't know what to DO there daily
prompt:
You are a LinkedIn activity coach. Create a simple, 10-minute daily routine that builds professional relationships through LinkedIn engagement.

My goal: Build relationships with [TARGET PROFESSIONALS — e.g., "lenders, attorneys, financial advisors"] in [MY MARKET] so they think of me when clients need an agent.

Design the daily 10-minute routine:

**Minutes 1-3: Scroll and React**
- How many posts to engage with: [number]
- What types of posts to prioritize
- The difference between a "like" (weak) and a "comment" (strong)

**Minutes 3-7: Comment Meaningfully**
- Write 5 example comments for common post types from professionals:
  - A lender shares a rate update
  - An attorney shares a legal tip
  - A financial advisor shares market commentary
  - A local business celebrates an anniversary
  - A professional shares a personal win
- Each comment: under 30 words, adds value or shows genuine interest

**Minutes 7-10: Direct Outreach**
- Send 1 DM or connection request per day
- The "engagement ladder": like 3x → comment 2x → DM 1x (don't DM someone you've never engaged with)

Example of a GOOD comment:
"This is such an important point about estate planning timelines. I see families hit by this all the time on the real estate side. Would love to hear more about how you counsel clients on this."

Example of a BAD comment:
"Great post! Thanks for sharing!"

Do NOT spend more than 10 minutes — set a timer.
Do NOT engage with content just to be seen — engage because you have something to add.
Do NOT use LinkedIn's auto-generated "Congrats!" messages — write your own.
quickStart: Give me a 3-step, 10-minute daily LinkedIn routine for a real estate agent building referral partner relationships. Include 2 example comments.

title: Build a LinkedIn Newsletter for Professional Positioning
bestFor: ** Agents who want long-term authority building and inbound partner interest
prompt:
You are a newsletter strategy consultant. Help me launch a LinkedIn Newsletter that positions me as a knowledgeable professional that other professionals want to partner with.

My market: [YOUR MARKET]. My expertise areas: [LIST 2-3]. My target reader: [PROFESSIONAL TYPES — e.g., "lenders, attorneys, financial advisors, and other agents"].

Create:

1. **Newsletter name and tagline** — 3 options. Each name under 5 words, tagline under 15 words. Should appeal to professionals, not consumers.

2. **Content pillars** — 4 recurring themes/sections for each edition:
   - [What goes in each section — described in 1-2 sentences]
   - Why professionals would read it

3. **First edition outline** — a complete structure for edition #1:
   - Subject line (under 60 characters)
   - Opening paragraph (under 75 words — write it)
   - Main section topic and 3 subpoints
   - "Partner Spotlight" section — how to feature a referral partner without it being an ad
   - Closing call-to-action (not "hire me" — something relationship-building)

4. **Publishing cadence** — recommended frequency and day/time, with reasoning.

5. **Growth strategy** — 5 ways to get subscribers among professional audiences (not just clients).

Total newsletter length target: under 600 words per edition (3-minute read).

Do NOT create a newsletter about listings or market reports for consumers — this is B2B positioning.
Do NOT publish more than twice a month — quality and consistency beat frequency.
Do NOT forget the "reply to this newsletter" CTA — direct responses build relationships.
quickStart: Give me a LinkedIn Newsletter name, tagline, and 4 content sections for a real estate agent who wants to attract referral partners (lenders, attorneys, advisors), not clients.

title: 24-Hour Post-Event Follow-Up System
bestFor: ** Agents who meet great people but never follow up
prompt:
You are a follow-up systems designer. Create the exact process I follow in the 24 hours after a networking event so no connection falls through the cracks.

I typically meet [NUMBER] people at a networking event. My current follow-up rate is: [PERCENTAGE — or "close to zero"].

Design my 24-hour post-event protocol:

**Within 1 hour (at the event or in the parking lot):**
- The 30-second "data dump" for each person I met — what to type into my phone:

| Name | How We Met | Personal Detail | Professional Detail | Follow-Up Idea | Priority (A/B/C) |

**Within 4 hours (same evening):**
- For "A" contacts (high potential): Write a text message template (under 30 words) that references something specific from our conversation.
- For all contacts: Send LinkedIn connection requests with a personalized note.

**Within 24 hours (next day):**
- For "A" contacts: Write an email template (under 75 words) that provides VALUE — not "great to meet you," but something useful.
- For "B" contacts: Write a shorter email template (under 50 words).
- For "C" contacts: LinkedIn connection with personalized note (already done).

Write 3 different "value-add" email templates:
1. "You mentioned [TOPIC] — here's [ARTICLE/RESOURCE] I thought of"
2. "You should meet [PERSON] — I think you'd really hit it off"
3. "I looked into [THING WE DISCUSSED] — here's what I found"

Do NOT write follow-ups that are just "Great meeting you!" — those get ignored.
Do NOT send the same template to everyone — the personalization IS the point.
Do NOT skip the data dump step — if you don't capture it in 60 minutes, it's gone.
quickStart: Give me a 3-step post-networking-event follow-up process: what to do at the event, that evening, and the next morning. Include 1 text and 1 email template.

title: Follow-Up Messages That Get Responses
bestFor: ** Agents who follow up but get ghosted
prompt:
You are a message optimization specialist. Write networking follow-up messages that actually get replied to.

The problem: My follow-up messages currently get [LOW / NO] response rate. I think it's because: [CHOOSE — "they're too generic," "they're too long," "I don't offer anything valuable," "I wait too long to send them"].

Write 8 follow-up message templates organized by channel:

**Text Messages (4 templates, each under 40 words):**
1. Same-day follow-up after meeting at a mixer
2. Day-after follow-up with a value offer
3. Follow-up to confirm a coffee meeting discussed at the event
4. Follow-up referencing a personal detail they shared

**Emails (4 templates, each under 100 words):**
5. The "resource share" — I'm sending something useful
6. The "introduction offer" — I want to connect them with someone
7. The "coffee invitation" — suggesting a 1-on-1 meeting
8. The "callback" — referencing an ongoing conversation topic

For each template:
- Subject line (for emails)
- The message
- Why it works (1 sentence)

CRITICAL RULE: Every message must give before it asks. Lead with value, not with a request.

Example of a GOOD text:
"Hey Mark, great talking tonight. That brewery you mentioned — my wife and I tried it last year and loved the stout. Let's grab a beer there sometime."

Example of a BAD text:
"Hi Mark, great meeting you tonight at the Chamber event. I'd love to stay in touch. Here's my card info..."

Do NOT start any message with "I hope this finds you well."
Do NOT include your full signature with every credential in a text message.
Do NOT write more than one follow-up if they don't respond — move on gracefully.
quickStart: Write 2 follow-up text messages (under 30 words each) for after a networking event. One that shares something useful, one that references a personal detail. Both should feel like a friend texting.

title: "Reconnect with Cold Contacts" Campaign
bestFor: ** Agents with a pile of old business cards and dead LinkedIn connections
prompt:
You are a relationship reactivation specialist. Help me reconnect with networking contacts I've let go cold.

I have approximately [NUMBER] professional contacts I met months or years ago but never followed up with (or stopped following up). They are: [DESCRIBE — "old business cards," "LinkedIn connections I never messaged," "people I met at events last year"].

Create:

1. **The "honest reconnection" message** — for people I clearly dropped the ball with. Under 75 words. Honest, self-aware, not over-apologetic.

Example: "Hey [NAME], I realize we connected at [EVENT] back in [MONTH] and I never followed up properly. Completely on me. I was thinking about [SOMETHING RELEVANT TO THEM] and your name came up. Would love to grab coffee if you're open to it."

2. **The "event-based reconnection"** — using an upcoming event as the excuse. Under 60 words.

3. **The "referral-based reconnection"** — I'm reaching out because I have someone they should meet. Under 60 words.

4. **The "content-based reconnection"** — sharing an article or insight relevant to their world. Under 60 words.

5. **The triage system** — how to prioritize which cold contacts to revive:

| Priority | Criteria | Action | Expected Response Rate |
|----------|----------|--------|----------------------|
| High | Met in person, had good conversation, professional relevance | Personal message | |
| Medium | Connected online, some engagement history | Lighter touch | |
| Low | Can't remember the context | Don't bother | |

Do NOT apologize excessively — one brief acknowledgment is enough.
Do NOT send mass emails — each reconnection must be individual.
Do NOT expect everyone to respond — focus on the ones who do.
quickStart: Write a reconnection message (under 50 words) to a professional contact I met 6 months ago and never followed up with. Honest, not cringey.

title: Relationship Development Ladder
bestFor: ** Agents who make contacts but can't turn them into real partnerships
prompt:
You are a relationship development strategist. Map the journey from "just met" to "active referral partner" with specific actions at each stage.

Design a 5-stage relationship development ladder:

**Stage 1: Contact (We exchanged info)**
- Duration: Week 1
- Actions required (list 3 specific ones)
- Messages to send (write 1 template, under 40 words)
- Graduation criteria: What signals they're ready for Stage 2?

**Stage 2: Acquaintance (We've had a real conversation)**
- Duration: Weeks 2-4
- Actions required (list 3)
- Messages to send (write 1 template, under 50 words)
- Graduation criteria

**Stage 3: Connection (We meet one-on-one, I understand their business)**
- Duration: Months 1-3
- Actions required (list 3)
- The coffee meeting goal (1 sentence)
- Graduation criteria

**Stage 4: Relationship (Regular communication, mutual trust)**
- Duration: Months 3-6
- Actions required (list 3)
- The referral conversation: how and when to formalize (write the script, under 50 words)
- Graduation criteria

**Stage 5: Partnership (Active referral flow, strategic collaboration)**
- Duration: Month 6+
- Maintenance actions (list 3)
- The annual review conversation (2-3 discussion points)

Also include:
- **Exit criteria** at each stage — when to stop investing in a relationship that's not progressing
- **The timeline reality check** — how long this typically takes (spoiler: 6-12 months for most partnerships)

Do NOT present this as a linear process — some relationships skip stages.
Do NOT make every stage about getting closer to referrals — the relationship has to be genuine.
Do NOT suggest rushing any stage — trust can't be accelerated.
quickStart: Give me the 5 stages of turning a networking contact into an active referral partner, with 1 key action at each stage and the typical timeline.

title: Group Follow-Up — The Monthly Partner Update Email
bestFor: ** Agents who want to stay top-of-mind with many professional contacts simultaneously
prompt:
You are a professional communications writer. Help me create a monthly email update specifically for my referral partner network — not for clients.

My network includes approximately [NUMBER] professional contacts (lenders, attorneys, financial advisors, agents in other markets, etc.).

Create:

1. **The format template** for a monthly "Partner Pulse" email:
   - Subject line formulas (3 options, under 50 characters each)
   - Structure:
     * Quick market stat that matters to THEIR clients (2-3 sentences)
     * 1 opportunity I can offer (a type of referral I'm looking for, a client need I can't fill)
     * 1 opportunity I'm sharing (a lead, an event, a resource)
     * A personal note (1-2 sentences, something human)
   - Total length: under 200 words

2. **A complete sample edition** (under 200 words) — ready to customize and send.

3. **3 subject lines that get opened:**
   - One curiosity-driven
   - One value-driven
   - One personal

4. **Sending logistics:**
   - Recommended send day/time
   - How to personalize at scale (segments, merge fields)
   - When to stop sending to unresponsive contacts

Do NOT make this a real estate newsletter for consumers — it's a brief, insider update for professionals.
Do NOT include listings, testimonials, or "how's the market?" general content.
Do NOT send this weekly — monthly is the right cadence for partner updates.
quickStart: Write a 150-word monthly email update for my referral partner network (lenders, attorneys, advisors). Include a market stat, an opportunity, and a personal note.

title: Referral Partner Quarterly Value Report
bestFor: ** Agents who want to strengthen existing referral partnerships by showing (not just telling) the value exchanged
prompt:
You are a relationship management strategist. Create a quarterly value report template I send to my top referral partners — a simple, professional update that shows what we've accomplished together and keeps the partnership strong.

My situation: I have [NUMBER] active referral partners. My top partner is a [PROFESSIONAL TYPE — e.g., "mortgage lender"]. This past quarter, approximately: [DESCRIBE — e.g., "I sent them 3 referrals, they sent me 2, we closed 2 deals together"].

Create a **Quarterly Value Report** template with these sections:

1. **Partnership Snapshot** (a simple table):
| Metric | This Quarter | Year-to-Date |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Referrals I sent you | ___ | ___ |
| Referrals you sent me | ___ | ___ |
| Deals closed together | ___ | ___ |
| Total client value created | ___ | ___ |

2. **Highlights** — 2-3 sentences noting specific wins from the quarter. Write these as templates with blanks: "[CLIENT NAME]'s closing went smoothly because [SPECIFIC THING PARTNER DID]." Make each one acknowledge the partner's contribution specifically.

3. **Upcoming Opportunities** — A brief section (3-4 sentences) sharing:
   - Clients in my pipeline who may need their services soon (with permission)
   - Market trends that could create referral opportunities
   - Any events or co-marketing I'm planning that involves them

4. **Genuine Thank-You** — A 2-3 sentence closing that is specific, warm, and not generic. NOT "Thanks for your partnership this quarter." Instead, reference one specific thing they did that mattered. Write 3 versions:
   - For a lender partner
   - For an attorney partner
   - For a general professional partner

5. **Format Options** — Provide the report in two formats:
   - A polished email version (under 250 words total)
   - A casual text/voice message version (under 50 words) for partners where email feels too formal

Do NOT make this feel like a corporate annual report — it should feel like a thoughtful note from a valued colleague.
Do NOT include data the partner already knows (like their own transaction volume). Focus on the partnership data.
Do NOT skip the thank-you section. This is the part that makes the partner feel valued, not just tracked.
quickStart: Write a 150-word email I can send to my top referral partner (a [PROFESSIONAL TYPE]) at the end of the quarter. Include: referrals exchanged, one specific win we had together, one upcoming opportunity, and a genuine thank-you. Keep it warm and professional.

title: Follow-Up Sequences for Different Contact Types
bestFor: ** Agents who want different follow-up strategies for different types of contacts
prompt:
You are a relationship sequencing expert. Design different follow-up cadences based on the TYPE of contact I made at a networking event.

Create 30-day follow-up sequences for 4 contact types:

**Type A: High-Potential Referral Partner (lender, attorney, advisor)**
Day 1: [Action + message template, under 40 words]
Day 3: [Action + message template]
Day 7: [Action]
Day 14: [Action + message template]
Day 30: [Action + message template]

**Type B: Fellow Agent (potential referral exchange)**
Day 1: [Action + message template, under 40 words]
Day 7: [Action]
Day 30: [Action + message template]

**Type C: Community Connector (business owner, organization leader)**
Day 1: [Action + message template, under 40 words]
Day 14: [Action]
Day 30: [Action + message template]

**Type D: Casual Contact (might be useful someday)**
Day 1: [Action — LinkedIn connection only]
Day 30: [Decision: engage or archive]

For each sequence:
- Specify the channel (text, email, LinkedIn, phone)
- Write actual message templates
- Include the "stop" signal — when to stop following up if they're not engaging

Do NOT make any sequence more than 5 touchpoints in 30 days — that's aggressive.
Do NOT use the same channel for consecutive touchpoints — mix it up.
Do NOT continue a sequence if someone hasn't responded to 2 consecutive messages.
quickStart: Give me a 3-step, 14-day follow-up sequence for a mortgage lender I met at a networking event. Include the message for each step.

title: "Value Bomb" Follow-Up — Providing Something Useful
bestFor: ** Agents who know they should "provide value" but don't know what that means practically
prompt:
You are a value creation coach for networkers. Help me identify and deliver specific "value bombs" — follow-up gestures that make networking contacts think "Wow, this person is genuinely helpful."

My market: [YOUR MARKET]. My areas of knowledge: [LIST — e.g., "neighborhoods, school districts, home values, local restaurants, contractors"]. My network includes: [TYPES OF PROFESSIONALS].

Create:

1. **15 "value bomb" ideas** organized by type:

**Information Gifts (5):**
- Specific, useful things I can share with different professionals. Describe each in 1-2 sentences.

**Introduction Gifts (5):**
- Types of introductions I can make that benefit the recipient. Each described with the exact message template (under 50 words) for making the introduction.

**Opportunity Gifts (5):**
- Ways I can send business, leads, or exposure to my contacts. Each described practically.

2. **The "value match" framework** — 5 questions to ask myself after meeting someone that help me identify what specific value I can provide THEM:
   - What are they trying to grow?
   - What problem did they mention?
   - Who do they need to meet?
   - What information would help their clients?
   - What would make their job easier?

3. **3 complete "value bomb" follow-up emails** — ready to customize. Under 75 words each.

Do NOT suggest generic "value" like "share an article" — be specific about WHAT article and WHY.
Do NOT recommend value that costs significant money — time and knowledge are the currency.
Do NOT provide value only to get something back — genuine generosity is the strategy.
quickStart: Give me 5 specific things a real estate agent can offer a new networking contact that provide genuine value. Not vague — practical and immediate.

title: "Warm Introduction" Email Templates
bestFor: ** Agents who want to be connectors but don't know how to make proper introductions
prompt:
You are a professional introduction specialist. Write email templates for introducing two people in my network who should know each other — the ultimate networking power move.

Create 5 introduction email templates for different scenarios:

1. **Introducing a lender to a buyer I'm not working with** — someone I met who needs financing but isn't my client. Under 75 words.

2. **Introducing two professionals who could benefit each other** — e.g., a CPA and a financial advisor. Under 75 words.

3. **Introducing a new contact to an established one** — vouching for someone I recently met. Under 75 words.

4. **Introducing a business owner to a potential customer** — connecting two people in my network. Under 75 words.

5. **Introducing two agents in different markets** — for potential referral exchange. Under 75 words.

Each email must:
- Address both people (CC'd or group message)
- Explain WHY I'm connecting them in 1 sentence
- Give each person a "hook" — why the other person is worth knowing
- End with "I'll let you two take it from here" (or similar — let them drive)

Example:
"Subject: Two people who should know each other

Hi Sarah, meet David. David, meet Sarah.

Sarah is a financial advisor who works with a lot of young families planning their first home purchase. David is a mortgage lender who specializes in first-time buyer programs.

I think you'd find a lot of overlap in your client base. I'll let you two take it from here — but I have a feeling this intro will pay off for both of you.

Best, [Me]"

Do NOT write introductions that feel like you're doing a favor — frame it as two people who genuinely should know each other.
Do NOT introduce people without asking permission from at least one party first.
Do NOT make yourself the center of the introduction — step back after connecting them.
quickStart: Write a 4-sentence email introducing two professionals who should know each other. Include why each person is worth meeting.

title: Weekly Relationship Maintenance Routine
bestFor: ** Agents who build relationships but let them die from neglect
prompt:
You are a relationship systems designer. Create a weekly routine that keeps my professional network warm without consuming my entire calendar.

I have approximately [NUMBER] professional contacts I want to maintain relationships with. I can spend [NUMBER] minutes per day on relationship maintenance.

Design a Monday-Friday routine (one focused activity per day):

**Monday: Review & Plan**
- What to check in my CRM or contact list
- Who to prioritize this week (selection criteria)
- Time: 10 minutes

**Tuesday: Share Value**
- What to share and with whom (market insight, article, resource)
- Write 3 template messages for sharing value (under 40 words each)
- Time: 15 minutes

**Wednesday: Make Connections**
- 1 introduction to make this week
- The introduction email template (under 60 words)
- Time: 10 minutes

**Thursday: Engage Digitally**
- LinkedIn/Instagram engagement targets (number of meaningful comments)
- 3 example comments that build relationships
- Time: 10 minutes

**Friday: Personal Touch**
- 1 personal message to a contact I haven't spoken with recently
- Write 5 "thinking of you" message templates (under 30 words each) that don't feel generic
- Time: 5 minutes

Total weekly time: under 60 minutes.

Do NOT suggest daily tasks that take more than 15 minutes — consistency beats intensity.
Do NOT include client follow-up in this routine — this is for professional network maintenance only.
Do NOT make any day feel like a chore — each task should feel like a small, good thing.
quickStart: Give me a 5-day weekly routine (1 task per day, under 10 minutes each) for maintaining professional relationships. Include a message template for each day.

title: Connector Reputation — Becoming the Person Who Knows Everyone
bestFor: ** Agents who want to be the go-to connector in their professional community
prompt:
You are a network influence strategist. Help me build a reputation as "the person who knows everyone" — the ultimate networking advantage for a real estate agent.

My current network: [DESCRIBE — size, types of people, depth]. My market: [YOUR MARKET].

Create:

1. **The "connector mindset" framework** — 5 daily habits that train me to think "who should meet whom?" in every interaction. Each habit: 1-2 sentences, practical.

2. **The introduction tracking system** — a simple format for logging connections I make:
| Date | Person A | Person B | Why I Connected Them | Outcome (if known) |

3. **5 "connector moves" I can make this month** — specific types of introductions I should be looking for. Describe each with a real-world example scenario.

4. **The "connector language"** — 5 phrases I naturally work into conversations that signal I'm a connector:
   Example: "Oh, you need a contractor? I actually know someone great — let me text you their info."
   Write 5 similar phrases for different situations.

5. **The long game** — how being a connector generates referrals over 12-24 months, even when individual introductions don't lead to immediate business.

Do NOT confuse being a connector with being a people-pleaser — connectors are strategic.
Do NOT introduce people who shouldn't be introduced — include 3 criteria for when NOT to make an intro.
Do NOT expect credit for every connection — the reputation builds silently.
quickStart: Give me 3 "connector habits" I can start this week as a real estate agent — small actions that make people think "they always know the right person to call."

title: Relationship Depth Assessment — Audit Your Network
bestFor: ** Agents with many contacts who don't know which relationships are actually strong
prompt:
You are a relationship analytics consultant. Help me audit the health of my professional network so I know where to invest my time.

I have approximately [NUMBER] professional contacts. I think [NUMBER] are active relationships and [NUMBER] have gone cold. I haven't formally assessed my network before.

Create:

1. **The Relationship Depth Scorecard** — a 1-10 rating system:

| Score | Level | Definition | Example Behavior |
|-------|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| 1-2 | Contact | We've met. I have their info. | They'd recognize my name in an email |
| 3-4 | Acquaintance | We've had a real conversation | They'd say hi at an event |
| 5-6 | Connection | We meet periodically | They'd take my call |
| 7-8 | Relationship | Regular communication, mutual trust | They'd refer a client to me |
| 9-10 | Partnership | Active referral flow, deep trust | They actively look for ways to send me business |

2. **The audit process** — a step-by-step method for scoring my top 30 contacts:
   - What questions to ask myself about each person
   - How long this should take (target: under 45 minutes)
   - How to record the results

3. **The "gap analysis"** — after the audit, 5 questions to identify where my network is weak:
   - Do I have enough people at Level 7+?
   - Are certain professional categories missing?
   - Which relationships have dropped and could be recovered?
   - Where am I over-invested?
   - What's my next priority?

4. **Action plan template** — for each contact scored 5-6, write a 1-sentence action to move them to 7-8.

Do NOT make this feel like judging people — it's about understanding where to invest time.
Do NOT score based on how much THEY give me — score based on relationship depth.
Do NOT audit more than 30 contacts at once — start with the most important.
quickStart: Give me a 5-level relationship depth scale for professional contacts, with the definition and one example behavior at each level. Help me score my top 10 contacts.

title: "Board of Advisors" — Building Your Personal Advisory Group
bestFor: ** Experienced agents who want a strategic support system beyond casual networking
prompt:
You are an executive coaching consultant. Help me assemble a personal "Board of Advisors" — a small group of professionals who help me grow strategically.

I've been in real estate [NUMBER] years. My current support system is: [DESCRIBE — mentor, coach, peer group, or "mostly just me"]. I want a structured advisory group that pushes me to grow.

Design:

1. **The 5 seats on my board:**

| Seat | Role | Who to Look For | What They Give Me | What I Give Them |
|------|------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | The Mentor | | | |
| 2 | The Peer | | | |
| 3 | The Specialist | | | |
| 4 | The Challenger | | | |
| 5 | The Connector | | | |

Fill in each column with specific descriptions.

2. **The invitation script** (under 100 words) — how to ask someone to be on my "board" without making it weird. This needs to feel like an honor, not a burden.

3. **The meeting format:**
   - Frequency: [recommendation with reasoning]
   - Duration: [recommendation]
   - Agenda structure (3-4 items, with time allocations)
   - The accountability component

4. **The "rules of engagement"** — 4 ground rules for making this group work long-term (confidentiality, candor, commitment, reciprocity).

Do NOT make this formal or corporate — it's a small group of trusted advisors, not a board of directors.
Do NOT suggest people who are too busy to actually meet regularly — realistic candidates.
Do NOT skip the "what I give them" column — this has to be mutual.
quickStart: Describe the 5 types of people a real estate agent should have in their personal advisory group, and what each person contributes. One sentence each.

title: Annual Networking Strategy Review
bestFor: ** End of year/quarter planning for agents with established networks
prompt:
You are a strategic planning facilitator. Help me review my networking performance and plan for the next 12 months.

My numbers this year:
- Networking events attended: [NUMBER]
- New professional contacts made: [NUMBER]
- Coffee/one-on-one meetings held: [NUMBER]
- Referrals received from professionals: [NUMBER]
- Referrals SENT to professionals: [NUMBER]
- Deals closed from networking referrals: [NUMBER]
- Revenue from networking-sourced business: [$AMOUNT]
- Estimated time spent on networking per week: [HOURS]
- Estimated money spent on networking this year: [$AMOUNT]

Run my networking audit:

1. **The Numbers** — calculate my key metrics in a table:
| Metric | This Year | Assessment |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Cost per networking-sourced deal | | |
| Hours per networking-sourced deal | | |
| Referral send/receive ratio | | |
| Contacts-to-referrals conversion rate | | |

2. **The Honest Assessment** — based on my numbers, give me a candid 3-paragraph evaluation: What's working? What's not? What's the biggest opportunity I'm missing?

3. **Next Year's Plan** — 5 specific changes or initiatives for the coming year, based on my data. For each: the action, the expected impact, and the quarterly milestone.

4. **The "stop doing" list** — based on my numbers, 2-3 networking activities I should CUT to free up time for higher-ROI activities.

Do NOT sugarcoat the assessment — I need honest analysis.
Do NOT recommend adding more activities without cutting others first. Time is finite.
Do NOT set goals that aren't connected to my actual numbers — data-driven only.
quickStart: If I attended [NUMBER] networking events this year and received [NUMBER] referrals, is my networking strategy working? Give me 3 metrics to calculate and an honest assessment.

title: Write a Compelling MLS Listing Description
prompt:
You are a real estate copywriter who specializes in MLS listing descriptions that sell lifestyle, not just features.

Write an MLS listing description for this property:

- Address: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths: [BEDS]/[BATHS]
- Square footage: [SQFT]
- Lot size: [LOT SIZE]
- Year built: [YEAR]
- Top 5 features: [LIST FEATURES]
- Neighborhood/location highlights: [NEIGHBORHOOD DETAILS]
- Target buyer: [e.g., young family, downsizer, investor]

Rules:
- Keep it under [WORD LIMIT, e.g., 250] words
- Open with a hook that highlights lifestyle, NOT the address or bed/bath count
- Weave in 2-3 sensory details (what you see, hear, feel)
- End with a clear call to action
- Do NOT use "boasts," "nestled," "won't last long," or "a must-see"
- Do NOT start sentences with "This home"
- Use present tense throughout

Output format: One polished paragraph block, ready to paste into MLS.
quickStart: You are a real estate copywriter. Write a 200-word MLS description for a [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [SQFT] sqft home at [ADDRESS] priced at [PRICE]. Top features: [FEATURES]. Open with a lifestyle hook. Do NOT use "boasts," "nestled," or "won't last long." End with a call to action.

title: Luxury Property Description
prompt:
You are a luxury real estate copywriter whose work appears in Robb Report and Mansion Global.

Write a luxury MLS listing description for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths: [BEDS]/[BATHS]
- Square footage: [SQFT]
- Architectural style: [e.g., Modern Mediterranean, Mid-Century Modern]
- Luxury features: [LIST 5-7 HIGH-END FEATURES]
- Views/setting: [DESCRIBE]
- Designer/builder (if notable): [NAME or N/A]

Rules:
- 200-300 words
- Tone: Understated elegance. Suggest exclusivity without being pretentious
- Lead with the most emotionally compelling element (view, architecture, privacy)
- Reference one lifestyle moment (e.g., "morning coffee overlooking..." or "entertain beneath...")
- DO NOT use "prestigious," "exquisite," "one-of-a-kind," or "palatial"
- DO NOT list features like a spec sheet — weave them into a narrative
- Include one sentence about the location's cachet

Output format: Polished description block, ready for MLS or luxury print brochure.
quickStart: You are a luxury real estate copywriter. Write a 250-word MLS description for a [PRICE] luxury home at [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [SQFT] sqft, [ARCHITECTURAL STYLE]. Key features: [FEATURES]. Tone: understated elegance. Lead with the most emotional element. Do NOT use "prestigious," "exquisite," or "one-of-a-kind."

title: Starter Home / First-Time Buyer Description
prompt:
You are a real estate copywriter who helps first-time buyers picture themselves in a home.

Write an MLS description for this starter home:

- Address: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths: [BEDS]/[BATHS]
- Square footage: [SQFT]
- Key features: [LIST 3-5 FEATURES]
- Neighborhood perks: [e.g., walkable to shops, good school district, near transit]
- Recent updates (if any): [UPDATES]

Rules:
- 150-200 words
- Emphasize value, convenience, and pride of ownership
- Highlight what makes this a smart first purchase (location, low maintenance, potential equity)
- Tone: Encouraging, warm, straightforward — not salesy
- DO NOT use language that sounds like you are apologizing for the home's size or price
- DO NOT use "cozy" as a euphemism for small — find genuine positives
- End with a sentence that helps the buyer envision daily life here

Output format: Single paragraph, MLS-ready.
quickStart: Write a 175-word MLS description for a starter home at [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [SQFT] sqft, priced at [PRICE]. Features: [FEATURES]. Tone: warm and encouraging, not salesy. Emphasize value and pride of ownership. Do NOT use "cozy" as a euphemism for small.

title: Condo or Townhouse Description
prompt:
You are a real estate copywriter experienced with condo and townhouse listings.

Write an MLS description for:

- Address/Unit: [ADDRESS AND UNIT NUMBER]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths: [BEDS]/[BATHS]
- Square footage: [SQFT]
- Floor/level: [e.g., 3rd floor corner unit, end-unit townhome]
- HOA fee: [AMOUNT] — includes: [WHAT HOA COVERS]
- Community amenities: [LIST]
- Unit-specific features: [e.g., balcony, in-unit laundry, parking type]
- Target buyer: [e.g., urban professional, downsizer, investor]

Rules:
- 150-200 words
- Address the HOA value proposition naturally (what the buyer gets for the fee)
- Highlight the unit's position advantage (corner, top floor, end unit, etc.)
- Reference at least one community amenity in context, not as a list
- DO NOT lead with the HOA fee or restrictions
- DO NOT use "low-maintenance living" — find a fresher angle
- Mention proximity to one key local destination

Output format: MLS-ready paragraph.
quickStart: Write a 175-word MLS description for a [FLOOR/LEVEL] condo at [ADDRESS], [PRICE]. [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [SQFT] sqft. HOA: [AMOUNT] includes [WHAT IT COVERS]. Amenities: [LIST]. Highlight the unit's position advantage and weave in one community amenity naturally. Do NOT lead with HOA or use "low-maintenance living."

title: Vacant Land Description
variables: WHAT IS BEING BUILT OR EXISTS NEARBY
prompt:
You are a real estate copywriter who specializes in land and development parcels.

Write an MLS description for this vacant land listing:

- Location: [ADDRESS or AREA DESCRIPTION]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Acreage: [ACRES]
- Zoning: [ZONING TYPE]
- Utilities available: [LIST — water, sewer, electric, gas, well, septic]
- Topography: [flat, rolling, wooded, hillside, waterfront, etc.]
- Access: [paved road, gravel, easement, etc.]
- Permitted uses: [residential, agricultural, commercial, mixed]
- Nearby development: [WHAT IS BEING BUILT OR EXISTS NEARBY]

Rules:
- 150-200 words
- Help the buyer see the potential — paint a vision of what could be built
- Lead with the land's most compelling characteristic (views, location, size, zoning flexibility)
- Include practical details buyers need (utilities, access, zoning) woven naturally into the copy
- DO NOT use "blank canvas" — it is overused for land listings
- DO NOT ignore practical concerns; land buyers are detail-oriented
- Mention one specific use-case scenario relevant to the zoning

Output format: MLS-ready paragraph.
quickStart: Write a 175-word MLS description for [ACRES] acres of vacant land at [LOCATION], priced at [PRICE]. Zoning: [ZONING]. Utilities: [LIST]. Topography: [DESCRIPTION]. Paint a vision of what could be built, include practical details buyers need, and do NOT use "blank canvas."

title: Price Reduction MLS Update
prompt:
You are a real estate marketing strategist who knows how to reposition a price reduction as a buying opportunity.

Rewrite the MLS description for a listing that just had a price reduction:

- Address: [ADDRESS]
- Original price: [ORIGINAL PRICE]
- New price: [NEW PRICE]
- Days on market: [DAYS]
- Original description (paste below): [PASTE ORIGINAL DESCRIPTION]
- Any new updates or improvements since listing: [LIST or "None"]
- Reason for reduction (for your context only — do not state in copy): [REASON]

Rules:
- 150-250 words
- Rewrite the opening to create renewed urgency without desperation
- Reframe the price as a value opportunity, not a markdown
- If there are new updates or improvements, highlight them prominently
- Refresh at least 30% of the original copy so it reads as new, not edited
- DO NOT mention "price reduced," "motivated seller," or "bring all offers"
- DO NOT reference the original price or days on market in the description
- Maintain a confident, positive tone throughout

Output format: Complete replacement MLS description, ready to paste.
quickStart: Rewrite this MLS description to reframe a price reduction as a buying opportunity. New price: [NEW PRICE]. Original description: [PASTE]. Refresh the opening, keep it 200 words, and do NOT mention "price reduced," "motivated seller," or the original price. Tone: confident and positive.

title: New Listing Announcement Post
prompt:
You are a social media copywriter for a real estate agent.

Write a new listing announcement post for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Top 3 features: [LIST]
- Neighborhood: [NEIGHBORHOOD NAME]
- Platform: [Instagram / Facebook / Both]

Rules:
- Open with a hook line that creates curiosity (question, bold statement, or "imagine this" scenario)
- 80-120 words for the body
- Include 3-5 relevant hashtags at the end
- Add a clear CTA (DM me, link in bio, call to schedule, etc.)
- DO NOT write "Just listed!" as the opening — find a more creative hook
- DO NOT use more than 2 exclamation marks total
- Tone: Enthusiastic but professional

Output format:
- Hook line (separated for emphasis)
- Body paragraph
- CTA line
- Hashtags
quickStart: Write a 100-word Instagram post announcing a new listing at [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [PRICE]. Features: [FEATURES]. Start with a curiosity hook, not "Just listed!" Include 5 hashtags and a CTA. Keep it professional, not salesy.

title: Just Sold Post That Generates Seller Leads
prompt:
You are a real estate social media strategist who turns "Just Sold" posts into seller lead magnets.

Write a Just Sold post for:

- Property: [ADDRESS or NEIGHBORHOOD — no full address needed]
- Sale details: [SOLD ABOVE/AT/BELOW asking, DAYS ON MARKET, MULTIPLE OFFERS if applicable]
- What you did differently: [YOUR MARKETING STRATEGY — e.g., staged, professional video, targeted ads]
- Platform: [Instagram / Facebook]

Rules:
- 80-120 words
- Structure: Celebrate the result, briefly credit your strategy, pivot to a CTA for homeowners considering selling
- The post should make other homeowners think "I want those results"
- DO NOT share the exact sale price (unless client approved)
- DO NOT make the post only about you — center the client's win
- DO NOT bash other agents or imply competitors would have done worse
- End with a soft CTA aimed at potential sellers, not buyers

Output format:
- Opening celebration line
- Brief strategy mention (1-2 sentences)
- Seller-focused CTA
- 3-5 hashtags
quickStart: Write a 100-word Instagram "Just Sold" post for a home in [NEIGHBORHOOD]. It sold [ABOVE/AT/BELOW asking] in [DAYS] days. My strategy: [STRATEGY]. Celebrate the client's win, briefly mention the strategy, then pivot to a CTA aimed at homeowners considering selling. Do NOT share the exact price or bash other agents.

title: Coming Soon Teaser Campaign (3-Post Series)
prompt:
You are a social media content strategist who builds anticipation for real estate listings.

Create a 3-post "Coming Soon" teaser campaign for:

- Property type: [e.g., 4-bed colonial, luxury condo, mid-century ranch]
- Neighborhood: [NEIGHBORHOOD]
- Price range: [PRICE RANGE]
- 3 standout features (reveal one per post): [FEATURE 1] / [FEATURE 2] / [FEATURE 3]
- Launch date: [DATE THE LISTING GOES LIVE]
- Platform: [Instagram / Facebook]

Rules:
- Post 1 (Day -5): Mysterious tease — hint at the neighborhood and one feature without revealing the property
- Post 2 (Day -3): Reveal a second feature and the price range — build excitement
- Post 3 (Day -1): Final reveal with key details and how to get first access
- Each post: 60-90 words
- Each post should end with a CTA that drives DMs or sign-ups for early access
- DO NOT reveal the full address until Post 3
- DO NOT use "Stay tuned" — use more compelling language
- Maintain a consistent voice across all three posts

Output format: Three clearly labeled posts (Post 1, Post 2, Post 3) with suggested posting day.
quickStart: Create a 3-post "Coming Soon" teaser campaign for a [PROPERTY TYPE] in [NEIGHBORHOOD], price range [PRICE RANGE]. Reveal one feature per post: [FEATURE 1], [FEATURE 2], [FEATURE 3]. Post 1 is mysterious, Post 2 reveals more, Post 3 is the full reveal. Each post 60-90 words. Do NOT use "Stay tuned."

title: Open House Promotion Post
prompt:
You are a social media copywriter for real estate open house promotions.

Write an open house announcement post for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Date/Time: [DAY, DATE, TIME RANGE]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- One unique reason to visit: [e.g., renovated kitchen, stunning backyard, best street in the neighborhood]
- Platform: [Instagram / Facebook]

Rules:
- 60-100 words
- Lead with a reason to attend, not "Open House this Sunday"
- Make it feel like an event, not just a viewing
- Include the practical details (date, time, address) clearly — do not bury them
- DO NOT use "Stop by!" or "Don't miss out!"
- DO NOT write more than 2 sentences before giving the date/time
- Include a CTA to save, share, or tag a friend

Output format:
- Attention-grabbing hook
- Event details (date, time, address)
- Brief property highlight
- CTA
- 3-4 hashtags
quickStart: Write an 80-word Instagram open house post for [ADDRESS] on [DATE/TIME]. Price: [PRICE], [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba. Highlight: [ONE UNIQUE FEATURE]. Lead with a reason to attend, not "Open House this Sunday." Include date/time clearly, a CTA to tag a friend, and 3-4 hashtags.

title: Price Improvement Social Post
prompt:
You are a real estate social media writer who knows how to frame price adjustments as buyer opportunities.

Write a price improvement post for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- New price: [NEW PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Best feature of the home: [FEATURE]
- Neighborhood highlight: [HIGHLIGHT]
- Platform: [Instagram / Facebook]

Rules:
- 60-100 words
- Frame this as a "new opportunity" or "fresh look," not a discount or desperation move
- Lead with what the buyer gets at this price, not the fact that it dropped
- DO NOT use "price reduced," "price drop," "reduced!!" or similar markdown language
- DO NOT mention the previous price
- DO NOT suggest the seller is motivated or eager
- Tone: Confident, matter-of-fact, opportunity-focused

Output format:
- Hook emphasizing value
- 2-3 sentences on the property
- CTA
- 3-4 hashtags
quickStart: Write an 80-word social post for a listing at [ADDRESS] now offered at [NEW PRICE] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba. Best feature: [FEATURE]. Frame it as a fresh opportunity. Do NOT use "price reduced" or mention the old price. Tone: confident, not desperate. Include a CTA and 3-4 hashtags.

title: Video Walkthrough Script for Social Media
variables: LIST KEY ROOMS AND THEIR STANDOUT FEATURES
WHO THIS HOME IS PERFECT FOR
prompt:
You are a real estate video content strategist who writes scripts for property walkthrough videos.

Write a 60-second video walkthrough script for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Room-by-room highlights: [LIST KEY ROOMS AND THEIR STANDOUT FEATURES]
- Target buyer: [WHO THIS HOME IS PERFECT FOR]
- Platform: [Instagram Reels / TikTok / YouTube Shorts]

Rules:
- Script should be 130-160 words (roughly 60 seconds spoken)
- Open with a hook in the first 3 seconds (question, bold claim, or surprising fact)
- Structure: Hook > Front door/exterior > Main living spaces > Kitchen > Primary suite > Outdoor/bonus space > Close with CTA
- Write in a conversational, first-person tone as if the agent is speaking to camera
- Include [PAUSE] markers where the visuals should breathe
- DO NOT script it like a formal listing description — it should sound natural when spoken aloud
- DO NOT say "As you can see" — the viewer can already see
- End with a CTA that works for the platform (follow, comment, DM)

Output format:
- Scene-by-scene script with visual direction notes in [brackets]
- Suggested on-screen text overlay for the hook
quickStart: Write a 60-second video walkthrough script for [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [PRICE]. Room highlights: [ROOMS AND FEATURES]. Open with a 3-second hook, walk through the home conversationally, and end with a CTA. Include [PAUSE] markers. Do NOT say "As you can see." Platform: [Instagram Reels / TikTok].

title: New Listing Email Blast to Your Database
prompt:
You are a real estate email marketing specialist.

Write a new listing email blast for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Top 5 features: [LIST]
- Neighborhood: [NEIGHBORHOOD]
- Open house date (if scheduled): [DATE or "TBD"]
- Agent name: [YOUR NAME]
- Agent phone: [PHONE]

Rules:
- Write 3 subject line options (under 50 characters each)
- Email body: 120-180 words
- Structure: Brief lifestyle-focused intro > bullet-point highlights > CTA
- Include a P.S. line that adds urgency or a personal touch
- DO NOT open with "I am excited to announce" or "I just listed"
- DO NOT include more than 6 bullet points
- Tone: Professional, personal, not mass-marketing

Output format:
- 3 subject line options
- Email body with greeting, content, CTA, signature block, and P.S.
quickStart: Write a new listing email for [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [PRICE]. Features: [TOP FEATURES]. Include 3 subject line options under 50 characters, a 150-word body with a lifestyle intro and bullet-point highlights, and a P.S. line. Do NOT open with "I'm excited to announce." Agent: [YOUR NAME], [PHONE].

title: Broker-to-Broker Listing Promotion Email
prompt:
You are a real estate marketing writer who understands agent-to-agent communication.

Write a broker-to-broker email promoting a listing:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Commission offered: [BUYER AGENT COMMISSION or "per MLS"]
- Key selling points: [3-4 FEATURES]
- Showing instructions: [e.g., "Schedule via ShowingTime," "Call listing agent"]
- Special incentives (if any): [e.g., broker open, bonus, etc.]
- Your name/brokerage: [NAME / BROKERAGE]

Rules:
- Write 2 subject line options (direct and professional)
- Email body: 100-150 words
- Lead with what makes this listing easy to sell (features their buyers want)
- Clearly state showing instructions and any incentives
- Tone: Collegial, direct, professional — agent to agent
- DO NOT be overly salesy — other agents see through it
- DO NOT bury the showing instructions
- Include a one-line invitation to call with questions

Output format:
- 2 subject line options
- Email body
quickStart: Write a broker-to-broker email promoting a listing at [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [PRICE]. Key features: [FEATURES]. Showing instructions: [INSTRUCTIONS]. 2 subject lines, 120-word body. Tone: collegial and direct. Do NOT be salesy. From: [YOUR NAME / BROKERAGE].

title: Open House Invitation Email
prompt:
You are a real estate email writer who creates open house invitations that feel personal, not mass-produced.

Write an open house invitation email for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Date/Time: [DAY, DATE, TIME RANGE]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- One compelling reason to attend: [UNIQUE FEATURE OR DRAW]
- Refreshments or special touches: [e.g., coffee bar, local bakery treats, or "N/A"]
- Agent name: [YOUR NAME]

Rules:
- Write 2 subject line options
- Email body: 80-120 words
- Make the open house feel like an event worth attending, even for non-buyers (neighbors = future seller leads)
- Clearly display date, time, and address so they are scannable
- DO NOT write a wall of text — use short paragraphs and white space
- DO NOT use "You're invited to..." as the opening — find something warmer
- Include a "forward to a friend" CTA

Output format:
- 2 subject line options
- Email body with clear event details, brief property teaser, and CTA
quickStart: Write an open house invitation email for [ADDRESS] on [DATE/TIME] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [PRICE]. Unique draw: [FEATURE]. 2 subject lines, 100-word body. Make it feel personal, not mass-produced. Display date/time/address prominently. Do NOT open with "You're invited." Include a "forward to a friend" CTA. From: [YOUR NAME].

title: Price Reduction Email to Interested Buyers
prompt:
You are a real estate email strategist who re-engages interested buyers after a price adjustment.

Write a price reduction notification email for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- New price: [NEW PRICE]
- Previous price: [PREVIOUS PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Key feature to re-highlight: [FEATURE]
- Any updates since original viewing: [e.g., new paint, carpet, landscaping, or "None"]
- Agent name: [YOUR NAME]

Rules:
- Write 2 subject line options
- Email body: 100-140 words
- Acknowledge they looked at or asked about the property previously
- Frame the new price as a reason to take a second look
- If there are physical updates, mention them — it justifies a new visit
- DO NOT say "motivated seller," "must sell," or "bring any offer"
- DO NOT write more than one sentence about the price change itself
- End with a specific CTA (schedule a second showing, call to discuss)

Output format:
- 2 subject line options
- Email body with personal tone
quickStart: Write an email to buyers who previously viewed [ADDRESS], now priced at [NEW PRICE] (was [PREVIOUS PRICE]). [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba. Feature to re-highlight: [FEATURE]. 2 subject lines, 120-word body. Acknowledge their previous interest, frame the new price as a reason for a second look, and end with a CTA to schedule a showing. Do NOT say "motivated seller."

title: Weekly or Monthly Market Update Newsletter
prompt:
You are a real estate content strategist who writes market update newsletters that people actually read.

Write a market update newsletter for:

- Market/area: [CITY, NEIGHBORHOOD, or ZIP CODE]
- Time period: [MONTH/QUARTER/WEEK]
- Key stats (provide what you have):
  - Median home price: [PRICE]
  - Price change vs. prior period: [+/- PERCENT]
  - Average days on market: [DAYS]
  - Inventory level: [NUMBER OF HOMES or "low/moderate/high"]
  - Interest rate snapshot: [CURRENT RATE]
- One local trend or story: [e.g., new development, school rezoning, business opening]
- Agent name and contact: [NAME / PHONE / EMAIL]

Rules:
- Write 2 subject line options
- Newsletter body: 200-280 words
- Structure: Brief engaging intro > 3-4 key stats with one-sentence plain-English interpretation each > local trend/story > CTA
- Translate every stat into what it means for the reader ("what this means for you")
- DO NOT dump a table of numbers without context
- DO NOT use jargon like "absorption rate" without explaining it
- Tone: Trusted advisor, not salesperson
- End with a CTA to reply with questions or schedule a home value check

Output format:
- 2 subject line options
- Newsletter body with clear section breaks
quickStart: Write a market update newsletter for [CITY/NEIGHBORHOOD] covering [MONTH/QUARTER]. Stats: median price [PRICE], days on market [DAYS], inventory [LEVEL], rates [RATE]. Local trend: [TREND]. 2 subject lines, 250-word body. Translate each stat into plain English. Tone: trusted advisor. End with a CTA. From: [YOUR NAME].

title: Just Listed Email with Neighborhood Context
prompt:
You are a real estate email writer who sells neighborhoods as much as homes.

Write a "Just Listed" email that weaves in neighborhood context:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Top 3 home features: [LIST]
- Neighborhood name: [NEIGHBORHOOD]
- 3 neighborhood highlights: [e.g., top-rated elementary school, Saturday farmers market, 10-minute commute to downtown]
- One "insider" detail only locals know: [e.g., the best coffee shop, the hidden park, the annual block party]
- Agent name: [YOUR NAME]

Rules:
- Write 2 subject line options
- Email body: 150-200 words
- Alternate between home features and neighborhood details — do not front-load one or the other
- The "insider" detail should feel personal and specific, not generic
- DO NOT write a neighborhood Wikipedia entry — keep it experiential
- DO NOT use "conveniently located near" — show, don't tell
- End with a CTA to schedule a showing or neighborhood tour

Output format:
- 2 subject line options
- Email body that blends home and neighborhood naturally
quickStart: Write a "Just Listed" email for [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [PRICE]. Home features: [FEATURES]. Neighborhood: [NEIGHBORHOOD]. Highlights: [3 NEIGHBORHOOD HIGHLIGHTS]. Insider detail: [LOCAL SECRET]. 2 subject lines, 175-word body. Alternate between home and neighborhood details. Do NOT use "conveniently located near." From: [YOUR NAME].

title: Property Flyer Headline and Copy
prompt:
You are a real estate print copywriter who writes high-impact flyer copy.

Write headline and body copy for a property flyer:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Top 5 features: [LIST]
- Target buyer: [WHO THIS HOME IS FOR]
- Agent name/brokerage/phone: [DETAILS]

Rules:
- Write 3 headline options (8 words or fewer each)
- Body copy: 60-80 words maximum (this is a flyer, not an essay)
- Bullet-point the top 5 features with brief descriptors (not full sentences)
- Include a one-line CTA
- DO NOT repeat the headline idea in the body copy
- DO NOT use headlines that could apply to any home ("Your Dream Home Awaits")
- Headlines should reference something specific about THIS property

Output format:
- 3 headline options
- Body copy paragraph (60-80 words)
- 5 feature bullets
- CTA line
quickStart: Write flyer copy for [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [PRICE]. Features: [TOP 5 FEATURES]. Give me 3 headlines (8 words max each), a 70-word body paragraph, 5 feature bullets, and a CTA line. Headlines must reference something specific about this property — no generic "Dream Home" lines. Agent: [NAME/PHONE].

title: Neighborhood Feature Sheet
prompt:
You are a real estate marketing writer creating a neighborhood feature sheet to accompany a listing.

Write copy for a neighborhood feature sheet:

- Neighborhood: [NEIGHBORHOOD NAME]
- City/Town: [CITY]
- Key demographics: [e.g., young families, professionals, retirees]
- Schools (with ratings if available): [LIST]
- Top 5 nearby amenities: [e.g., parks, restaurants, shopping, transit]
- Commute info: [DISTANCE/TIME TO MAJOR EMPLOYMENT CENTERS]
- Median home price in area: [PRICE]
- One thing that makes this neighborhood special: [UNIQUE SELLING POINT]

Rules:
- Total copy: 150-200 words across all sections
- Use these section headers: "The Neighborhood" / "Schools" / "Eat, Shop & Play" / "Getting Around" / "Why Residents Love It"
- Each section: 2-4 concise sentences or bullet points
- Write like a knowledgeable local, not a guidebook
- DO NOT include negative comparisons to other neighborhoods
- DO NOT fabricate or estimate school ratings — if not provided, omit ratings
- Keep all copy scannable — this will be read in 30 seconds at an open house

Output format: Copy organized under the 5 section headers listed above.
quickStart: Write a neighborhood feature sheet for [NEIGHBORHOOD] in [CITY]. Schools: [LIST]. Amenities: [LIST]. Commute: [INFO].
