What makes it special: [UNIQUE POINT]. 175 words total across 5 sections: The Neighborhood, Schools, Eat/Shop/Play, Getting Around, Why Residents Love It. Write like a local, not a guidebook. Keep it scannable.

title: Luxury Listing Brochure Copy
prompt:
You are a luxury real estate brochure copywriter who creates editorial-quality property narratives.

Write brochure copy for a luxury listing:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Architectural style: [STYLE]
- Designer/Architect (if notable): [NAME or N/A]
- Lot/land: [ACREAGE AND DESCRIPTION]
- Luxury features: [LIST 6-8 FEATURES]
- Location/views: [DESCRIBE SETTING]
- Lifestyle the home enables: [e.g., indoor-outdoor entertaining, equestrian, waterfront living]

Rules:
- Write 3 sections:
  1. "The Residence" (80-100 words) — architectural narrative and interior highlights
  2. "The Grounds" (50-70 words) — outdoor spaces, landscaping, views
  3. "The Location" (50-70 words) — area prestige, proximity to cultural/recreational attractions
- Tone: Magazine editorial. Evocative but restrained
- Use one carefully placed metaphor or sensory detail per section
- DO NOT use "sprawling," "exquisite," "unparalleled," or "world-class"
- DO NOT write in bullet points — this should read as prose
- Every sentence should earn its place; cut anything generic

Output format: Three labeled sections of polished prose, ready for brochure layout.
quickStart: Write luxury brochure copy for [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [SQFT] sqft, [PRICE]. Style: [ARCHITECTURAL STYLE]. Features: [LUXURY FEATURES]. Setting: [VIEWS/LOCATION]. Three sections: "The Residence" (90 words), "The Grounds" (60 words), "The Location" (60 words). Tone: magazine editorial. Do NOT use "sprawling," "exquisite," or "world-class." Prose only, no bullets.

title: Investment Property Listing Description
prompt:
You are a real estate copywriter who specializes in investment property listings that speak the language investors care about: returns, cash flow, and upside.

Write an MLS listing description for this investment property:

- Address: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Property type: [e.g., single-family rental, duplex, triplex, fourplex, small multifamily]
- Units: [NUMBER OF UNITS]
- Beds/Baths per unit: [BREAKDOWN — e.g., "Unit 1: 2bd/1ba, Unit 2: 1bd/1ba"]
- Square footage: [TOTAL SQFT]
- Current rental income: [MONTHLY GROSS RENT or "vacant — market rent est. $X"]
- Lease status: [e.g., month-to-month, lease through MM/YYYY, vacant]
- Cap rate (if known): [CAP RATE or "calculate based on expenses"]
- Annual expenses (taxes, insurance, maintenance estimate): [AMOUNTS or "available upon request"]
- Recent capital improvements: [LIST or "None"]
- Neighborhood/market context: [e.g., rental demand, vacancy rates, nearby employers, university proximity]
- Upside potential: [e.g., below-market rents, value-add renovation opportunity, ADU potential, zoning allows expansion]

Rules:
- 150-250 words
- Lead with the investment thesis — why this property makes financial sense
- Include specific numbers (rent, cap rate, expenses) naturally in the copy, not as a data dump
- Highlight upside potential without making income projections or guarantees
- Mention tenant status and lease terms factually
- DO NOT use emotional lifestyle language ("picture yourself...") — investors buy spreadsheets, not sunsets
- DO NOT use "cash cow," "money maker," or "set it and forget it"
- DO NOT guarantee returns or project future income
- Include one sentence on the neighborhood's rental market strength
- End with a CTA to request the full income/expense package

Output format: MLS-ready description with a clear investor focus.
quickStart: Write a 200-word MLS description for an investment property at [ADDRESS] — [PROPERTY TYPE], [UNITS] units, [PRICE]. Current rent: [MONTHLY RENT]. Cap rate: [CAP RATE]. Upside: [UPSIDE POTENTIAL]. Write for investors, not homebuyers — lead with the investment thesis, include specific numbers, and end with a CTA to request the full income/expense package. Do NOT use "cash cow" or guarantee returns.

title: Complete 7-Day Listing Launch Marketing Plan
prompt:
You are a real estate marketing strategist who builds comprehensive listing launch plans.

Create a 7-day listing launch marketing plan for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Top features: [LIST 3-5]
- Target buyer profile: [DESCRIBE]
- Agent's available channels: [e.g., MLS, Instagram, Facebook, email list of X contacts, agent network, print flyers]
- Budget for paid promotion: [$AMOUNT or "organic only"]
- Open house planned: [YES — DATE / NO]

Rules:
- Provide a day-by-day plan (Day 1 through Day 7)
- Each day: 1-2 specific actions with the channel, content type, and a one-sentence description of the message/angle
- Day 1 should be MLS entry + foundational content (photos, description)
- Build momentum through the week toward the open house or "best offers by" date
- DO NOT suggest channels the agent did not list as available
- DO NOT include generic advice ("post on social media") — be specific about what to post
- Include one creative or unexpected tactic (neighbor preview, local business cross-promotion, etc.)

Output format: Table with columns: Day | Channel | Action | Content Angle
quickStart: Create a 7-day listing launch plan for [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [PRICE]. Available channels: [YOUR CHANNELS]. Open house: [YES/NO + DATE]. Give me a day-by-day table (Day | Channel | Action | Content Angle) with 1-2 actions per day. Be specific — no generic "post on social media." Include one creative tactic.

title: Stale Listing Refresh Strategy (45+ Days on Market)
variables: WHAT HAS BEEN DONE SO FAR
prompt:
You are a real estate marketing consultant specializing in reviving listings that have gone stale.

Create a listing refresh strategy for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Current price: [PRICE]
- Original list price: [ORIGINAL PRICE]
- Days on market: [DAYS]
- Number of showings to date: [NUMBER]
- Feedback themes from showings: [SUMMARIZE COMMON FEEDBACK]
- Current marketing in place: [WHAT HAS BEEN DONE SO FAR]
- Seller's flexibility: [e.g., open to price adjustment, willing to stage, firm on price]
- Budget for refresh: [$AMOUNT]

Rules:
- Provide 5-7 specific, actionable recommendations ranked by likely impact
- For each recommendation: What to do, Why it works, Estimated cost, Timeline to implement
- Address the showing feedback directly — do not ignore it
- Include at least one no-cost tactic and one creative outside-the-box idea
- DO NOT recommend a price reduction as the only solution
- DO NOT suggest "better photos" unless the current photos are noted as a problem
- Be honest about what the feedback suggests without being discouraging

Output format: Numbered recommendations with What / Why / Cost / Timeline for each.
quickStart: My listing at [ADDRESS] has been on market [DAYS] days with [NUMBER] showings. Current price: [PRICE]. Showing feedback: [FEEDBACK THEMES]. Seller is [FLEXIBILITY LEVEL]. Give me 5-7 ranked recommendations to refresh this listing. For each: What to do, Why it works, Cost, Timeline. Do NOT just say "reduce the price." Include one no-cost idea and one creative tactic.

title: Virtual Tour Narration Script
prompt:
You are a real estate virtual tour narrator who guides viewers through a home with engaging, informative commentary.

Write a narration script for a virtual tour of:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Room-by-room details (list rooms in tour order):
  [ROOM 1]: [KEY FEATURES]
  [ROOM 2]: [KEY FEATURES]
  [ROOM 3]: [KEY FEATURES]
  (continue for all rooms)
- Architectural style: [STYLE]
- Outdoor spaces: [DESCRIBE]
- Target buyer: [WHO THIS HOME IS FOR]

Rules:
- Total script: 300-400 words (approximately 2-3 minutes narrated)
- Write a 1-2 sentence transition between each room
- Highlight one "hero" feature per room — do not narrate everything visible
- Include 2-3 lifestyle moments ("This is where you will..." or "Picture yourself...")
- Opening: Welcome and set expectations for the tour
- Closing: Recap the top 3 reasons to schedule an in-person visit
- DO NOT describe paint colors or obvious visual elements the viewer can see
- DO NOT rush — build in [PAUSE] markers for visual moments
- Tone: Friendly guide, not auctioneer

Output format: Scene-by-scene script with room labels and [PAUSE] markers.
quickStart: Write a 2-3 minute virtual tour narration script for [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [SQFT] sqft, [PRICE]. Rooms in order: [ROOM 1: FEATURES], [ROOM 2: FEATURES], [ROOM 3: FEATURES]. Highlight one hero feature per room, include [PAUSE] markers, and close with 3 reasons to visit in person. Tone: friendly guide. Do NOT describe paint colors.

title: 60-Second Property Video Script
variables: THE ONE THING THAT SELLS THIS HOME
prompt:
You are a real estate video producer who writes tight, cinematic property video scripts.

Write a 60-second property video script for:

- Property: [ADDRESS]
- Price: [PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Architectural style: [STYLE]
- Hero feature: [THE ONE THING THAT SELLS THIS HOME]
- 4 supporting features: [LIST]
- Setting/views: [DESCRIBE]
- Target buyer: [WHO THIS IS FOR]
- Music mood: [e.g., upbeat and modern, warm and cinematic, elegant and quiet]

Rules:
- Script should be 140-160 words (60 seconds spoken at natural pace)
- Structure:
  - 0-5 sec: Visual hook + text overlay (no voiceover)
  - 5-20 sec: Exterior and approach
  - 20-45 sec: Interior highlights (3-4 rooms max)
  - 45-55 sec: Hero feature showcase
  - 55-60 sec: End card with CTA and agent info
- Include camera direction notes in [brackets]
- Write voiceover copy that complements visuals, not describes them
- DO NOT narrate what the camera shows — add context, emotion, or story
- DO NOT try to cover every room — choose the most impactful spaces
- Leave 2-3 moments of "visual only, no voiceover" for cinematic breathing room

Output format: Timestamped script with [camera direction] and voiceover/text overlay clearly separated.
quickStart: Write a 60-second cinematic video script for [ADDRESS] — [BEDS]bd/[BATHS]ba, [PRICE], [STYLE]. Hero feature: [FEATURE]. Supporting features: [LIST]. Include timestamped segments (0-5s hook, 5-20s exterior, 20-45s interior, 45-55s hero feature, 55-60s CTA). Add [camera directions]. Music mood: [MOOD]. Do NOT narrate what the camera shows — add emotion and story instead.

title: Listing Presentation Pitch for Sellers
prompt:
You are a listing presentation coach who helps agents articulate their marketing value proposition to potential seller clients.

Write a marketing section for a listing presentation:

- Agent name: [YOUR NAME]
- Brokerage: [BROKERAGE]
- Marketing channels you use: [LIST — e.g., MLS, professional photography, drone, video, social media, email database of X, print, paid ads]
- Your unique marketing advantage: [WHAT SETS YOU APART — e.g., large social following, video expertise, neighborhood specialist]
- Average days on market for your listings vs. market average: [YOUR DOM / MARKET DOM]
- Property being pitched: [BRIEF DESCRIPTION]

Rules:
- Write 5-6 talking points the agent can use during the presentation
- Each talking point: 2-3 sentences max
- Frame every marketing tactic in terms of what it does for the SELLER (more exposure, faster sale, higher price)
- Include one specific example or statistic that builds credibility
- DO NOT bash other agents' marketing
- DO NOT promise specific results ("I guarantee...")
- DO NOT use industry jargon the seller will not understand
- Tone: Confident, specific, client-focused

Output format: Numbered talking points, ready to be spoken aloud or placed on a presentation slide.
quickStart: Write 5-6 listing presentation talking points for [YOUR NAME] at [BROKERAGE]. My channels: [LIST]. My advantage: [UNIQUE ADVANTAGE]. My DOM vs. market: [YOUR DOM / MARKET DOM]. Property: [DESCRIPTION]. Frame each tactic in terms of seller benefit. Do NOT bash competitors or promise specific results. 2-3 sentences per point.

title: Comparative Market Analysis Narrative
variables: ADDRESS, SOLD PRICE, SQFT, BEDS/BATHS, SOLD DATE, KEY DIFFERENCES
prompt:
You are a real estate analyst who translates market data into clear narratives that help sellers understand pricing strategy.

Write a CMA narrative summary for:

- Subject property: [ADDRESS]
- Suggested list price: [PRICE]
- Comparable sales (provide 3-5):
  - Comp 1: [ADDRESS, SOLD PRICE, SQFT, BEDS/BATHS, SOLD DATE, KEY DIFFERENCES]
  - Comp 2: [ADDRESS, SOLD PRICE, SQFT, BEDS/BATHS, SOLD DATE, KEY DIFFERENCES]
  - Comp 3: [ADDRESS, SOLD PRICE, SQFT, BEDS/BATHS, SOLD DATE, KEY DIFFERENCES]
- Subject property advantages over comps: [LIST]
- Subject property disadvantages vs. comps: [LIST]
- Current market conditions: [BUYER'S / SELLER'S / BALANCED]

Rules:
- 200-280 words
- Structure: Market context (2-3 sentences) > Comp analysis summary > Price recommendation with rationale
- Explain WHY each comp supports the suggested price — do not just list them
- Acknowledge the property's disadvantages honestly but frame them constructively
- DO NOT use overly technical language
- DO NOT present the price as the only option — offer a narrow range with reasoning
- DO NOT ignore the disadvantages; sellers respect honesty
- Tone: Trusted advisor explaining their professional recommendation

Output format: Narrative paragraphs with a clear price recommendation conclusion.
quickStart: Write a CMA narrative for [ADDRESS]. Suggested price: [PRICE]. Comps: [COMP 1: address, sold price, key differences], [COMP 2], [COMP 3]. Advantages over comps: [LIST]. Disadvantages: [LIST]. Market: [BUYER'S/SELLER'S/BALANCED]. 250 words. Explain why each comp supports the price, acknowledge disadvantages honestly, and offer a narrow price range with reasoning. Tone: trusted advisor.

title: Withdrawn & Relisted Strategy Copy
prompt:
You are a real estate marketing strategist who specializes in repositioning properties that come back on market after being withdrawn. You know how to address the elephant in the room — why it was pulled — while creating genuine renewed interest.

Write a complete withdrawn-and-relisted marketing package for:

- Address: [ADDRESS]
- New list price: [NEW PRICE]
- Previous list price: [PREVIOUS PRICE]
- Beds/Baths/SqFt: [BEDS]/[BATHS]/[SQFT]
- Days on market before withdrawal: [DAYS]
- Reason for withdrawal (for your context — be honest): [e.g., seller changed mind, needed repairs, priced too high, personal circumstances, waited for better market conditions, completed renovations]
- What has changed since withdrawal: [e.g., price adjustment, renovations completed, new staging, market conditions shifted, seller circumstances resolved]
- Key property features: [LIST 3-5 FEATURES]
- Target buyer: [WHO THIS HOME IS FOR]
- Agent: [YOUR NAME / BROKERAGE] — [same agent as before / new listing agent]

Provide 3 deliverables:

1. **New MLS description** (150-250 words):
- This must read as a completely fresh listing, not a revision of old copy
- Address the "back on market" status in the opening sentence — do not hide it or bury it in the middle
- Immediately pivot to what has changed and why now is the right time
- Highlight any improvements or updates made during the off-market period
- DO NOT use "second chance," "back by popular demand," or "don't miss it this time"
- DO NOT blame the previous situation or make excuses
- DO NOT reference the old price or old days on market
- Tone: Transparent, confident, forward-looking

2. **Agent-to-agent email** (100-150 words):
- Write 2 subject line options
- Acknowledge this is a relisted property — agents will check the history anyway
- Focus on what has changed and why it deserves a fresh showing from their buyers
- If you are a new listing agent, briefly mention the fresh perspective you bring
- Tone: Professional, straightforward, no spin
- End with showing instructions and a CTA to call with questions

3. **Social media post** (80-120 words):
- Platform: [Instagram / Facebook]
- Frame the relisting as a story of a home finding its right moment
- Be honest without being negative — "This home is back, and here is why that is good news for you"
- DO NOT pretend it is a brand-new listing — followers may remember it
- Include a CTA and 3-5 hashtags

Output format: Three clearly labeled deliverables, each ready to use.
quickStart: A listing at [ADDRESS] was withdrawn after [DAYS] days and is coming back at [NEW PRICE] (was [PREVIOUS PRICE]). What changed: [CHANGES]. Write three things: (1) a 200-word new MLS description that addresses "back on market" in the opening sentence then pivots to what has changed, (2) a 120-word agent-to-agent email with 2 subject lines, and (3) a 100-word social media post that frames the relisting honestly. Do NOT use "second chance" or pretend it is brand new.

title: Post-Closing "Your Home's Story" Content
prompt:
You are a real estate content writer who turns successful transactions into compelling story-based marketing content.

Write a "Your Home's Story" post-closing content piece:

- Property (neighborhood only, no address): [NEIGHBORHOOD]
- Property type: [e.g., 3-bed ranch, luxury condo, fixer-upper]
- Sale highlights: [e.g., sold in 5 days, 12 offers, above asking, record price for street]
- Seller's situation: [e.g., relocating, upsizing, estate sale, first-time seller]
- Challenge you overcame: [e.g., tough inspection, appraisal gap, needed fast closing, low inventory for their next home]
- Marketing tactics that worked: [WHAT DROVE THE RESULT]
- Client quote (if available): [QUOTE or "N/A"]
- Agent name: [YOUR NAME]

Rules:
- Create 2 versions:
  1. Social media post (100-130 words) — storytelling format with a clear takeaway for potential sellers
  2. Website/portfolio case study (180-250 words) — more detailed, structured as Challenge > Strategy > Result

- Focus on the story arc: problem, approach, outcome
- Highlight your role without being self-congratulatory — let the results speak
- If a client quote is provided, integrate it naturally
- DO NOT share confidential financial details unless generalized (e.g., "above asking" is fine, exact overage may not be)
- DO NOT fabricate results or exaggerate
- End both versions with a CTA relevant to potential sellers

Output format: Two labeled versions (Social Post and Case Study), each complete and ready to use.
quickStart: Write post-closing content for a [PROPERTY TYPE] in [NEIGHBORHOOD]. Sold [SALE HIGHLIGHTS]. Challenge: [CHALLENGE]. Strategy: [WHAT WORKED]. Client quote: [QUOTE or N/A]. Two versions: (1) a 120-word social media post with a storytelling arc and seller CTA, (2) a 220-word website case study structured as Challenge > Strategy > Result. Do NOT share exact financial details. Agent: [YOUR NAME].

title: Compelling Offer Summary for a Listing Agent
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents submitting offers in competitive situations
prompt:
You are a real estate communication specialist. I need you to write a compelling offer summary that I (the buyer's agent) will send to the listing agent alongside my buyer's formal offer.

Here are the offer details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- List price: [PRICE]
- Offer price: [PRICE]
- Financing type: [CONVENTIONAL / FHA / VA / CASH]
- Down payment: [AMOUNT or PERCENTAGE]
- Pre-approval status: [FULLY UNDERWRITTEN / PRE-APPROVED / PRE-QUALIFIED]
- Earnest money: [AMOUNT]
- Requested closing date: [DATE]
- Contingencies: [LIST THEM — inspection, appraisal, financing, sale of home, etc.]
- Any flexible terms: [e.g., flexible on closing date, rent-back offered, as-is, etc.]
- Buyer's strengths: [e.g., relocating for work so highly motivated, stable employment, etc.]

Write a professional summary (250-400 words) that highlights why this offer deserves serious consideration. Lead with the strongest terms. Address potential concerns the seller might have. Close with a confident statement about my buyer's commitment.

Tone: Professional, confident, and respectful. You are making a case, not begging.

Format the output as an email I can send directly to the listing agent.

Do NOT fabricate financial details or qualifications I haven't provided.
Do NOT use desperate or overly emotional language ("Please, please pick us!").
Do NOT disparage other potential offers or buyers.
quickStart: Write a professional email to a listing agent summarizing why my buyer's offer on [ADDRESS] at [PRICE] is strong. Offer details: [LIST KEY TERMS]. Tone: confident, not desperate. Format as a ready-to-send email.

title: Multi-Offer Strategy — Positioning Against 3+ Competing Offers
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents in competitive multiple-offer situations
prompt:
You are a real estate negotiation strategist. My buyer is competing against multiple offers on a property, and I need a strategy to position their offer as the strongest choice.

Situation details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- List price: [PRICE]
- Number of competing offers (if known): [NUMBER or "unknown"]
- Days on market: [NUMBER]
- My buyer's max budget: [AMOUNT]
- My buyer's financing: [TYPE]
- Contingencies my buyer is willing to waive or modify: [LIST]
- Seller's known priorities (if any): [e.g., quick close, rent-back, highest price, certainty of close]
- My buyer's unique advantages: [e.g., cash, large down payment, flexible timeline, pre-underwritten]

Provide a strategic recommendation that covers:
1. Recommended offer price and rationale (within my buyer's stated budget)
2. Which contingencies to modify or waive, with risk assessment for each
3. Non-price sweeteners that could differentiate the offer
4. How to structure earnest money to signal commitment
5. Recommended escalation clause (if appropriate) with cap and increment
6. What to include in the cover letter or agent summary

Present this as a numbered strategy brief I can walk through with my buyer. For each recommendation, include the upside AND the risk so my buyer can make an informed decision.

Do NOT recommend waiving contingencies without clearly stating the financial risk to the buyer.
Do NOT guarantee this strategy will win — no one can promise that.
Do NOT suggest any terms that could be interpreted as discriminatory under fair housing laws.
quickStart: My buyer is competing against [NUMBER] offers on a [PRICE] property. Budget max: [AMOUNT]. Financing: [TYPE]. What strategy should we use to win? Include price positioning, contingency modifications with risks, and non-price sweeteners. Format as a strategy brief.

title: Seller Net Sheet Explanation Email
bestFor: ** Listing agents explaining estimated net proceeds to sellers in plain language
prompt:
You are a real estate communication specialist. I need to send my seller a clear, jargon-free email walking them through their estimated net proceeds — what they will actually take home after all costs are deducted from the sale price.

Here are the details:
- Seller name(s): [NAME]
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Expected or contracted sale price: [PRICE]
- Estimated costs:
  - Listing agent commission: [AMOUNT or PERCENTAGE]
  - Buyer's agent commission (if offered): [AMOUNT or PERCENTAGE]
  - Title insurance (seller's policy): [AMOUNT]
  - Transfer taxes / recording fees: [AMOUNT]
  - Prorated property taxes: [AMOUNT]
  - HOA prorations or fees (if applicable): [AMOUNT]
  - Estimated repairs or seller credits: [AMOUNT]
  - Mortgage payoff (remaining balance): [AMOUNT]
  - Other costs: [LIST ANY — e.g., home warranty, pest inspection, attorney fees, outstanding liens]

Write a professional email (350-500 words) that:
1. Opens warmly — this is about empowering the seller with financial clarity, not overwhelming them
2. Lists each cost as a separate line item with the dollar amount
3. After EACH line item, includes a brief "What this means" explanation in plain language (1-2 sentences explaining what the cost is and why it exists)
4. Shows a clear subtotal of all costs
5. Calculates and highlights the estimated net proceeds (sale price minus total costs)
6. Notes that these are estimates and the final numbers will come from the title company's settlement statement
7. Invites questions — "If any of these numbers surprise you, let's talk through them"

Tone: Clear, transparent, empowering. The seller should feel informed and confident, not blindsided at the closing table.

Do NOT use industry jargon without explaining it — assume the seller has never seen a net sheet before.
Do NOT present these numbers as final — they are estimates subject to change.
Do NOT editorialize about whether any cost is "too high" or "a great deal."

Output format: Ready-to-send email with subject line.
quickStart: Write a net proceeds email for my seller [NAME] on [ADDRESS]. Sale price: [PRICE]. Costs: commission [AMOUNT], title [AMOUNT], transfer taxes [AMOUNT], repairs [AMOUNT], mortgage payoff [AMOUNT]. Explain each line item in plain language, show the estimated net, and note these are estimates. Clear, jargon-free.

title: Counter-Offer Response Strategy
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents responding to a seller's counter-offer
prompt:
You are a real estate negotiation coach. The seller has countered my buyer's offer, and I need to develop a response strategy and draft communication.

Original offer details:
- Original offer price: [PRICE]
- Seller's counter price: [PRICE]
- Other counter terms changed: [e.g., closing date moved, contingency period shortened, seller wants to exclude fixtures, added rent-back period, etc.]
- My buyer's max budget: [AMOUNT]
- Comparable sales supporting my buyer's price: [LIST 2-3 COMPS with addresses and sale prices]
- Days on market for this property: [NUMBER]
- Current market conditions: [BUYER'S MARKET / BALANCED / SELLER'S MARKET]
- My buyer's walk-away point: [PRICE or "not yet determined"]

Provide:
1. Analysis of the counter — is it reasonable given the market data?
2. Recommended counter-to-the-counter with rationale
3. Draft message to the listing agent presenting our response (professional, data-driven, collaborative tone)
4. Talking points for my conversation with my buyer about the counter
5. Identification of non-price terms where we might find middle ground

Format as two sections: (A) Internal strategy notes for me to review with my buyer, and (B) Draft email to the listing agent.

Do NOT be combative or dismissive of the seller's position in the listing agent communication.
Do NOT reveal my buyer's maximum budget or walk-away point in any communication to the listing agent.
Do NOT present opinions as facts — use the comp data I provided.
quickStart: Seller countered my buyer's [ORIGINAL PRICE] offer at [COUNTER PRICE] on [ADDRESS]. Comps support [PRICE RANGE]. Draft a counter-to-counter response and an email to the listing agent. Keep buyer's max budget confidential.

title: Buyer's Agent Value Pitch — Post-NAR Settlement
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents articulating their value to buyers questioning whether they need an agent
prompt:
You are a real estate business strategist specializing in buyer agency value communication. In the post-NAR settlement world, buyer agency compensation structures have changed, and some buyers are questioning whether they need an agent at all. I need help preparing for that conversation.

My details:
- My market: [CITY / STATE]
- My experience: [YEARS in business, NUMBER of transactions, any specialties]
- My specific value-adds: [e.g., strong negotiation track record, access to off-market listings, average savings below list price for my buyers, lender and inspector network, transaction coordination, post-NAR settlement expertise]
- Buyer's specific concern: [e.g., "Why do I need to pay an agent?", "Can't I just go directly to the listing agent?", "I can find homes online myself", "My friend bought without an agent"]
- My compensation structure: [DESCRIBE — e.g., buyer agency agreement at X%, negotiable, seller concession request, flat fee, etc.]

Create three things:

1. **Talking points** (10-12 bullet points) — concise, specific value statements I can use in conversation. Each should be one sentence that a buyer would immediately understand. Focus on what I DO, not what I AM.

2. **With-Agent vs. Without-Agent comparison** — a two-column comparison showing what the buyer gains and what they risk at each stage of the transaction:
   - Home search and selection
   - Offer strategy and writing
   - Negotiation
   - Inspection and due diligence
   - Appraisal issues
   - Closing coordination
   - Legal and contractual protection

3. **Conversation script** (400-500 words) — for the moment when a buyer says "Why do I need you?" or "Can't I just do this myself?" The script should:
   - Validate their question — it's smart to ask
   - Acknowledge the industry changes honestly (don't pretend nothing changed)
   - Walk through 3-4 specific scenarios where not having an agent costs real money or creates real risk
   - Use a concrete dollar example showing how negotiation skill affects their bottom line
   - Address the "go direct to the listing agent" myth
   - Close with confidence — not desperation

Tone: Confident, transparent, educational. You are a professional who earns their fee — not someone begging for business.

Do NOT disparage buyers who choose to go unrepresented — respect their right to choose.
Do NOT use fear tactics or exaggerate risks — honesty builds trust.
Do NOT claim all agents provide the same value — differentiate based on MY specific strengths and track record.

Output format: Three clearly labeled sections.
quickStart: Help me explain my value as a buyer's agent to a buyer who is questioning whether they need representation. My experience: [DETAILS]. Create talking points, a with-agent vs. without-agent comparison, and a conversation script for the "Why do I need you?" question. Post-NAR settlement context. Confident, not desperate.

title: Below-Asking Offer Rationale
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents submitting offers below list price with data-backed reasoning
prompt:
You are a real estate negotiation writer. My buyer wants to submit an offer below asking price, and I need a data-driven rationale to accompany the offer so the listing agent understands our reasoning rather than dismissing it.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- List price: [PRICE]
- Offer price: [PRICE]
- Percentage below asking: [CALCULATE OR PROVIDE]
- Days on market: [NUMBER]
- Price reductions (if any): [DATES AND AMOUNTS]
- Comparable sold properties: [LIST 3-5 COMPS — address, sale price, date sold, sq ft, condition notes]
- Property condition concerns: [e.g., roof age, outdated systems, needed repairs]
- Current market conditions: [BUYER'S MARKET / BALANCED / SELLER'S MARKET]
- Any other factors: [e.g., seasonal slowdown, interest rate impact, neighborhood trends]

Write two things:
1. A professional email to the listing agent (200-300 words) that presents the offer as thoughtful and data-informed, not lowball. Frame it as "here's how we arrived at this number" rather than "your listing is overpriced."
2. Talking points for my buyer so they understand the strategy and aren't embarrassed by the below-ask number.

Do NOT be condescending or imply the seller overpriced the home.
Do NOT cherry-pick only the lowest comps — present a balanced view.
Do NOT apologize for the offer — confidence matters.
quickStart: Write a professional email to a listing agent explaining why my buyer's offer of [OFFER PRICE] on a [LIST PRICE] property is reasonable. Include these comps: [LIST COMPS]. Tone: confident, data-driven, respectful. Not a lowball — a market-based offer.

title: Competing Against Cash Offers with Financing
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents whose financed buyers are competing against cash offers
prompt:
You are a real estate strategist. My buyer is using financing and competing against one or more cash offers. I need a strategy to make our financed offer as competitive as possible, plus a cover letter to the listing agent addressing the financing head-on.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- List price: [PRICE]
- My buyer's offer price: [PRICE]
- Financing type: [CONVENTIONAL / FHA / VA]
- Down payment amount: [AMOUNT / PERCENTAGE]
- Pre-approval level: [PRE-QUALIFIED / PRE-APPROVED / FULLY UNDERWRITTEN]
- Lender name and reputation (if notable): [NAME]
- Buyer's financial strengths: [e.g., excellent credit, large reserves, stable employment, no contingent sale]
- Contingencies my buyer will include: [LIST]
- Contingencies my buyer might waive or shorten: [LIST with buyer's comfort level]
- Appraisal gap coverage: [AMOUNT buyer can cover if appraisal comes in low, if any]

Provide:
1. A strategy brief outlining how to position this financed offer to compete with cash (bullet points, prioritized)
2. A cover letter to the listing agent (250-350 words) that reframes financing as a strength — addressing certainty of close, timeline, and buyer qualifications
3. Specific language addressing the seller's likely concerns: closing timeline, appraisal risk, and loan contingency

Do NOT promise the loan will close — no one can guarantee that.
Do NOT recommend the buyer waive the financing contingency unless you explain the catastrophic risk of doing so.
Do NOT pretend financing is "just as good as cash" — instead, demonstrate why the financing risk is minimal in this case.
quickStart: My buyer is using [LOAN TYPE] financing against cash offers on [ADDRESS]. Pre-approval level: [LEVEL]. Down payment: [AMOUNT]. Write a strategy to compete and a cover letter to the listing agent that addresses financing concerns head-on.

title: Inspection Repair Request — Prioritize 47 Items
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents organizing a lengthy inspection report into a strategic repair request
prompt:
You are a real estate negotiation strategist helping me prioritize inspection findings. My buyer's inspection report came back with a long list of items. I need help sorting them into categories so I can build a strategic repair request.

Inspection findings (paste or summarize the key items):
[LIST ALL ITEMS — e.g.:
- Roof: Missing shingles in 3 areas, estimated 5 years remaining life
- Electrical: Double-tapped breaker, ungrounded outlets in bedrooms
- HVAC: System is 18 years old, working but near end of life
- Plumbing: Slow drain in master bath, minor leak under kitchen sink
- Foundation: Hairline crack in basement wall (inspector noted "monitor")
- ...continue listing all items]

Property price: [PRICE]
Market conditions: [BUYER'S MARKET / BALANCED / SELLER'S MARKET]
Buyer's risk tolerance: [LOW — wants everything fixed / MODERATE — wants safety and big-ticket items / HIGH — only deal-breakers]

Sort every item into three categories:
1. **MUST-FIX (Non-negotiable):** Safety hazards, code violations, structural concerns, items that affect insurability or lender requirements
2. **NICE-TO-FIX (Request but flexible):** Functional issues, items nearing end of life, moderate-cost repairs
3. **SKIP (Don't request):** Cosmetic items, minor maintenance, items the buyer can handle post-closing

For each MUST-FIX item, explain in one sentence why it belongs in that category. Provide an estimated repair cost range if possible (note these are rough estimates — get contractor quotes for accuracy).

End with a recommended negotiation approach: ask for repairs, ask for credit, or a mix.

Do NOT diagnose problems — I have a licensed inspector's report for that.
Do NOT guarantee cost estimates — recommend getting contractor bids.
Do NOT include cosmetic preferences in the MUST-FIX category.
quickStart: Sort these inspection items into MUST-FIX, NICE-TO-FIX, and SKIP categories: [PASTE ITEMS]. Property price: [PRICE]. Market: [TYPE]. Focus MUST-FIX on safety, structural, code, and lender-required items.

title: Repair Request Email to the Listing Agent
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents submitting inspection repair requests
prompt:
You are a real estate communication specialist. I need to write the inspection repair request email to the listing agent. The goal is to be firm about what we need while keeping the deal collaborative and moving forward.

Property address: [ADDRESS]
Items we are requesting repairs or credit for:
[LIST ITEMS WITH DETAILS — e.g.:
1. Electrical panel: Double-tapped breakers (safety/code issue) — requesting licensed electrician repair
2. Roof: Missing shingles, 3 areas — requesting repair
3. Plumbing: Active leak under kitchen sink — requesting repair
4. HVAC: 18 years old, near end of life — requesting $[AMOUNT] credit toward replacement]

Total credit requested (if applicable): [AMOUNT]
Inspection report attached: Yes/No
Repair request addendum attached: Yes/No

Write a professional email (250-350 words) to the listing agent that:
- Opens by acknowledging the overall condition of the home (start positive if honest)
- Presents the requests as focused on safety, structural, and functional issues
- Distinguishes between "repair needed" and "credit requested" items
- Invites dialogue — this is a negotiation, not a demand
- Closes with a clear next step and timeline reference

Do NOT use threatening language or ultimatums ("fix everything or we walk").
Do NOT be apologetic about legitimate safety concerns.
Do NOT editorialize about the seller's maintenance habits.

Output format: Ready-to-send email with subject line.
quickStart: Write a professional email to the listing agent requesting inspection repairs on [ADDRESS]. Items: [LIST]. Total credit: [AMOUNT]. Tone: firm but collaborative — not aggressive, not apologetic. Include a subject line.

title: Low Appraisal Appeal Letter
bestFor: ** Listing agents or buyer's agents appealing a low appraisal with supporting data
prompt:
You are a real estate appraisal appeal specialist. The appraisal came in below the contract price, and I need to write a professional appeal letter (often called a "Reconsideration of Value" or ROV) to submit to the lender for the appraiser's review.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Contract price: [PRICE]
- Appraised value: [PRICE]
- Gap: [AMOUNT]
- Comps the appraiser used (if available): [LIST with addresses and values]
- Better comps I want the appraiser to consider: [LIST with addresses, sale prices, sale dates, sq ft, condition, and why they are more comparable]
- Property features the appraiser may have undervalued or missed: [e.g., recent renovations ($X invested), unique features, lot size advantages, neighborhood premium]
- Errors in the appraisal report (if any): [e.g., wrong square footage, wrong bedroom count, missed upgrades]

Write a professional ROV letter (400-600 words) that:
- Opens respectfully — this is a request for reconsideration, not a complaint
- Presents 3-5 comparable sales with clear data showing why they support a higher value
- Identifies specific errors or omissions in the original appraisal (if any)
- Highlights property improvements or features that may not have been fully weighted
- Avoids emotional arguments — sticks to data and facts
- Closes with a clear, specific value conclusion

Format: Professional letter suitable for submission through the lender to the appraiser. Include a header with property address, borrower name placeholder, and date.

Do NOT attack the appraiser's competence or judgment.
Do NOT include comps I haven't provided — you cannot access MLS data.
Do NOT present inflated or misleading data.
quickStart: Write an appraisal appeal letter for [ADDRESS]. Contract price: [PRICE]. Appraised at: [PRICE]. Better comps: [LIST WITH DETAILS]. Errors found: [IF ANY]. Professional tone, data-focused, respectful. Format as a formal ROV letter.

title: Low Appraisal — Message to the Seller
bestFor: ** Listing agents delivering low appraisal news to their seller
prompt:
You are a real estate communication specialist. The appraisal on my listing came in below the contract price, and I need to explain the situation and options to my seller clearly and calmly.

Details:
- Contract price: [PRICE]
- Appraised value: [PRICE]
- Gap amount: [AMOUNT]
- Buyer's position (if known): [e.g., buyer willing to split the difference, buyer asking seller to reduce, buyer can cover gap with cash, unknown]
- Appeal status: [e.g., we are filing an appeal, appeal was denied, not pursuing appeal]
- Market context: [any relevant info — multiple offers received, recent comps, etc.]

Write a message to my seller (300-400 words) that:
1. Delivers the news clearly and without sugarcoating
2. Explains what a low appraisal means and why it happens (briefly)
3. Presents ALL options available to the seller:
   - Reduce the price to appraised value
   - Negotiate a middle ground with the buyer
   - Buyer covers the gap (if applicable)
   - Cancel the contract and relist
   - Appeal the appraisal
4. Provides my honest assessment of which option makes the most sense given the circumstances
5. Ends with a clear call to action — "Let's schedule a call to discuss"

Tone: Calm, honest, solutions-oriented. The seller will be frustrated — validate that feeling without feeding it.

Do NOT blame the appraiser in a way that gives the seller false hope.
Do NOT pressure the seller toward any one option — present all paths.
Do NOT promise an appeal will be successful.

Output format: Email or text message (specify which you prefer: [EMAIL / TEXT]).
quickStart: The appraisal on my listing came in at [APPRAISED VALUE] vs. contract price of [CONTRACT PRICE]. Write a message to my seller explaining the situation, all options, and my recommended path forward. Tone: calm, honest, no sugarcoating. Format as [EMAIL / TEXT].

title: Major Inspection Issue — Message to the Buyer
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents delivering bad inspection news to their buyer
prompt:
You are a real estate communication specialist. My buyer's home inspection revealed a major issue, and I need to communicate it clearly while helping them understand their options without panicking.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Major issue found: [DESCRIBE — e.g., foundation crack requiring structural repair, active termite infestation, failing septic system, knob-and-tube wiring throughout, mold in crawl space]
- Inspector's characterization: [e.g., "significant concern," "recommend specialist evaluation," "immediate repair needed"]
- Estimated repair cost (if known): [AMOUNT or "unknown — needs specialist bid"]
- Does this affect safety, habitability, or insurability? [YES/NO and explain]
- Contract contingency status: [e.g., within inspection contingency period, how many days remain]
- Seller's likely willingness to negotiate: [ESTIMATE based on market conditions]

Write a message (300-400 words) to my buyer that:
1. Delivers the finding clearly and factually — no hedging, no dramatizing
2. Explains what the issue means in practical terms (cost, timeline, livability impact)
3. Presents their options:
   - Request the seller repair before closing
   - Request a credit to handle it themselves post-closing
   - Renegotiate the price to account for the issue
   - Terminate the contract using the inspection contingency
4. Gives an honest assessment — is this a deal-breaker or a negotiation opportunity?
5. Recommends a next step (e.g., get a specialist inspection, get contractor bids)

Do NOT minimize a serious issue to keep the deal alive.
Do NOT catastrophize a manageable problem.
Do NOT give advice about structural, environmental, or health risks — defer to specialists.

Output format: Email to my buyer.
quickStart: My buyer's inspection on [ADDRESS] found [MAJOR ISSUE]. Write an email explaining the issue, what it means practically, all options (repair request, credit, renegotiate, terminate), and recommended next step. Honest and calm tone.

title: Repair Credit Instead of Actual Repairs — The Pitch
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents proposing a credit in lieu of repairs
prompt:
You are a real estate negotiation writer. After the inspection, my buyer prefers a credit at closing rather than having the seller make repairs. I need to write a persuasive message to the listing agent explaining why a credit is better for everyone.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Inspection items at issue: [LIST with estimated costs]
- Total credit requested: [AMOUNT]
- Why my buyer prefers a credit: [e.g., wants to choose their own contractors, wants to handle repairs to their standard, doesn't trust rushed pre-closing repairs, wants to prioritize certain repairs over others]
- What's in it for the seller: [e.g., no hassle of coordinating repairs, no risk of contractor delays affecting closing, no additional costs beyond the credit amount, cleaner transaction]

Write a professional email (200-300 words) to the listing agent that:
1. Frames the credit as a win-win, not a demand
2. Explains why a credit benefits the seller (less hassle, more certainty)
3. Specifies the exact credit amount and what it covers
4. Maintains forward momentum — the goal is to keep the deal moving

Do NOT frame this as the buyer not trusting the seller's repairs (even if that's true).
Do NOT present a credit as the only option — show flexibility.
Do NOT inflate repair estimates to justify a larger credit.

Output format: Ready-to-send email with subject line.
quickStart: Write an email to the listing agent proposing a $[AMOUNT] credit at closing instead of repairs on [ADDRESS]. Items: [LIST]. Frame it as a win-win — easier for the seller, preferred by the buyer. Professional tone.

title: Walk-Away Analysis — Should My Buyer Terminate?
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents helping buyers evaluate whether to walk away based on inspection results
prompt:
You are a real estate decision-making advisor. My buyer is deciding whether to move forward with a purchase or terminate the contract based on the inspection results. I need a structured analysis to help them make an informed decision.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Contract price: [PRICE]
- Major inspection findings: [LIST each issue with estimated repair cost]
- Total estimated repair costs: [AMOUNT]
- Are any issues structural, environmental, or safety-related? [YES/NO — describe]
- How long has the buyer been searching? [MONTHS]
- Buyer's emotional attachment to this property: [LOW / MODERATE / HIGH]
- Current market conditions: [BUYER'S MARKET / BALANCED / SELLER'S MARKET]
- Availability of comparable properties: [SCARCE / MODERATE / PLENTIFUL]
- Earnest money at risk if they DON'T terminate within contingency period: [AMOUNT]
- Days remaining in inspection contingency: [NUMBER]
- Has the seller offered any concessions? [YES — describe / NO / NOT YET ASKED]

Create a decision framework with two columns:
**REASONS TO MOVE FORWARD** | **REASONS TO WALK AWAY**

Then provide:
1. A "true cost" calculation: Purchase price + estimated repairs = total investment. How does that compare to comparable properties?
2. Risk assessment: What's the worst-case scenario if the repairs end up costing more than estimated?
3. Opportunity cost: What does the buyer give up by walking away (time, market position, this specific property)?
4. My honest recommendation (with caveats)

Format as a structured document I can walk through with my buyer in a meeting or phone call.

Do NOT make the decision for the buyer — present the analysis and let them choose.
Do NOT minimize real risks to keep the deal alive.
Do NOT assume repair costs are fixed — note they could be higher.
quickStart: My buyer is considering walking away from [ADDRESS] at [PRICE]. Inspection issues total approximately $[AMOUNT] in repairs. Create a walk-away vs. move-forward analysis with true cost calculation, risk assessment, and my recommendation. Format as a decision document.

title: Price Reduction Conversation — Listing Sitting 30+ Days
bestFor: ** Listing agents advising a seller on a price reduction
prompt:
You are a real estate listing strategy advisor. My listing has been on the market for [NUMBER] days without an acceptable offer, and I need to have the price reduction conversation with my seller. Help me prepare.

Listing details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Original list price: [PRICE]
- Current list price (if already reduced): [PRICE]
- Days on market: [NUMBER]
- Number of showings: [NUMBER]
- Number of offers received: [NUMBER]
- Feedback from showings (summarize themes): [e.g., "priced too high for the condition," "love the location but kitchen is dated," "competing listings offer more for the price"]
- Current comparable active listings: [LIST 2-3 with prices]
- Recent comparable sold properties: [LIST 2-3 with sold prices]
- Suggested new price: [PRICE]
- Percentage reduction: [CALCULATE]

Write two things:
1. **Conversation script** (400-500 words) — talking points for my phone call or meeting with the seller. Structure it as:
   - Acknowledge their frustration and validate their feelings
   - Present the market data honestly
   - Explain why time on market matters (stigma, stale listing)
   - Present the recommended price and why
   - Address their likely objection: "But we put $X into renovations"
   - Close with confidence and a clear recommendation

2. **Follow-up email** (200-300 words) — sent after the conversation to document the recommendation in writing.

Do NOT blame the seller or say "I told you so."
Do NOT guarantee a quick sale at the new price.
Do NOT sugarcoat — if the market is speaking, the seller needs to hear it.
quickStart: My listing at [ADDRESS] has been on the market [NUMBER] days at [PRICE]. Showings: [NUMBER], offers: [NUMBER]. Comps support [LOWER PRICE]. Write talking points for the price reduction conversation with my seller and a follow-up email. Honest but empathetic.

title: "Your Home Isn't Worth What Zillow Says"
bestFor: ** Listing agents addressing Zestimate or AVM discrepancies with sellers
prompt:
You are a real estate communication coach. My seller is anchored to an online home value estimate (Zillow Zestimate, Redfin Estimate, etc.) that is higher than my professional market analysis. I need to address this tactfully while establishing my credibility.

Details:
- Online estimate the seller is citing: [AMOUNT and SOURCE]
- My CMA-based recommended price: [AMOUNT]
- Gap between online estimate and my recommendation: [AMOUNT]
- Why the online estimate is likely inaccurate for THIS property: [e.g., doesn't account for condition, uses inaccurate square footage, doesn't know about deferred maintenance, neighborhood micro-factors, recent comps tell a different story]
- 3 key comps that support my price: [LIST with addresses, sold prices, dates]
- The seller's likely emotional response: [e.g., insulted, skeptical, anchored to their number for financial reasons]

Write a conversation script (400-500 words) that:
1. Validates the seller's research — they're being informed, that's good
2. Explains how automated estimates work (and their known error margins)
3. Explains what a CMA accounts for that an algorithm cannot
4. Presents 3 specific comps with commentary
5. Bridges to my recommended price with confidence
6. Addresses the "but my neighbor sold for $X" objection

Tone: Respectful of the seller's intelligence. Not dismissive of online tools — just clear about their limitations. You are the local expert; the data is your ally.

Do NOT mock Zillow or online tools — many buyers use them too.
Do NOT be condescending about the seller's research.
Do NOT pretend your CMA is infallible either — acknowledge it's a professional opinion based on data.
quickStart: My seller thinks their home is worth [ONLINE ESTIMATE] based on Zillow. My CMA says [MY PRICE]. Gap: [AMOUNT]. Write a conversation script that respects their research but explains why online estimates miss key factors for their specific property. Include comps: [LIST].

title: Seller Wants to Overprice by $50K — Pre-Listing Conversation
bestFor: ** Listing agents navigating the initial pricing conversation when the seller has unrealistic expectations
prompt:
You are a real estate pricing strategist and communication coach. I'm preparing for a listing presentation where the seller wants to list their home significantly above what the market data supports. I need to persuade them without losing the listing.

Details:
- Seller's desired list price: [PRICE]
- My CMA-recommended price: [PRICE]
- Overprice gap: [AMOUNT]
- Why the seller wants the higher price: [e.g., "that's what we need to buy our next home," "our neighbor sold for that," "we put $X into renovations," "we just want to test the market"]
- Key comparable sales: [LIST 3-5 with addresses, sold prices, dates, key features]
- Current active competition: [LIST 2-3 active listings with prices that the seller would be competing against]
- Average days on market in this area/price range: [NUMBER]
- Market trend: [APPRECIATING / FLAT / DECLINING]

Write a persuasion strategy document that includes:
1. **Opening frame:** How to start the conversation (acknowledging their perspective)
2. **The data case:** How to present comps and market data compellingly
3. **The overpricing risk:** What happens to homes that sit — lost buyer interest, price reduction stigma, appraisal risk even if they find a buyer
4. **The "test the market" rebuttal:** Why this strategy usually backfires
5. **The renovation argument:** Why $1 spent does not equal $1 in value (with tact)
6. **My recommended pricing strategy:** Present a range and explain the strategy behind it
7. **The compromise:** If they insist, what is the maximum you can agree to while protecting your professional reputation? And what's the plan if it doesn't sell in [X] weeks?

Do NOT agree to an unrealistic price just to get the listing.
Do NOT make the seller feel stupid for their price expectations.
Do NOT guarantee any specific result at any price point.
quickStart: My seller wants to list at [SELLER'S PRICE]. Comps support [MY PRICE]. Gap: [AMOUNT]. Write a persuasion strategy for the pricing conversation — acknowledge their perspective, present the data case, explain overpricing risks, and offer a compromise if they insist. Professional but firm.

title: Explaining a Home's Price to a Buyer — Above or Below Comps
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents explaining pricing context to their buyers
prompt:
You are a real estate educator. My buyer is asking why a home is priced [ABOVE / BELOW] comparable recent sales, and I need to provide a clear, balanced explanation.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- List price: [PRICE]
- Comparable recent sales: [LIST 3-4 with addresses, sold prices, and key differences from the subject property]
- Why the price is [ABOVE / BELOW] comps: [e.g., recent renovations, premium lot, estate sale, needs major repairs, motivated seller, unique features, distressed sale, etc.]
- Is the price justified in your professional opinion? [YES / PARTIALLY / NO]

Write an explanation (250-350 words) in conversational language that:
1. Acknowledges the buyer's observation — good for them for comparing
2. Explains the specific factors that account for the price difference
3. Identifies which factors add real value vs. which are seller wishful thinking
4. Gives an honest assessment: is this fairly priced, overpriced, or a potential deal?
5. Recommends what to offer if the buyer is interested

Format as talking points I can use in a conversation with my buyer, or copy into an email.

Do NOT tell the buyer what to do — inform and advise, then let them decide.
Do NOT dismiss the seller's pricing without fair consideration.
Do NOT speculate about the seller's personal financial situation.
quickStart: My buyer is asking why [ADDRESS] is listed at [PRICE] when comps sold at [COMP RANGE]. Explain the likely reasons for the difference, whether the price is justified, and what I'd recommend offering. Conversational tone.

title: Multiple Offer Presentation to a Seller — Comparing 4 Offers
bestFor: ** Listing agents presenting and comparing multiple offers to their seller
prompt:
You are a real estate offer analysis specialist. I have received multiple offers on my listing and need to present them to my seller in a clear, organized, apples-to-apples comparison.

Listing details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- List price: [PRICE]

Offer details (provide for each):
**Offer 1:**
- Buyer: [IDENTIFIER — "Buyer A"]
- Offer price: [PRICE]
- Financing: [CASH / CONVENTIONAL / FHA / VA]
- Down payment: [AMOUNT or %]
- Earnest money: [AMOUNT]
- Contingencies: [LIST]
- Closing date: [DATE]
- Special terms: [e.g., escalation clause, appraisal gap coverage, rent-back, as-is, etc.]

[REPEAT FOR EACH OFFER]

Seller's stated priorities: [e.g., highest price, fastest close, fewest contingencies, certainty of close, flexibility on move-out date]

Create:
1. A **comparison table** showing all offers side-by-side across key terms
2. A **"net to seller" estimate** for each offer (accounting for price, concessions, estimated closing costs — note these are estimates)
3. A **risk assessment** for each offer (what could go wrong — appraisal, financing, contingencies)
4. A **ranked recommendation** based on the seller's stated priorities, with a brief explanation for each ranking
5. **Questions to ask** the seller before deciding (what tradeoffs matter most to them?)

Do NOT recommend based solely on price — certainty of close matters.
Do NOT share any offer's details with another buyer or agent.
Do NOT advise the seller to violate any fair housing laws in their selection.

Output format: A structured presentation document I can print or screen-share with my seller.
quickStart: I have [NUMBER] offers on my listing at [ADDRESS] listed at [PRICE]. Create a comparison table, net-to-seller estimates, risk assessment, and ranked recommendation. Offers: [LIST KEY TERMS FOR EACH]. Seller's top priority: [PRIORITY].

title: "Best and Final" Request to Buyer Agents
bestFor: ** Listing agents requesting highest and best offers in a multiple-offer situation
prompt:
You are a real estate communication specialist. I have received multiple offers on my listing and, with my seller's authorization, I am requesting "highest and best" (best and final) offers from all parties. I need a professional email to send to each buyer's agent.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Number of offers received: [NUMBER]
- Deadline for best and final submissions: [DATE and TIME]
- Any specific terms the seller values beyond price: [e.g., closing timeline flexibility, waived contingencies, appraisal gap coverage — or "price is the primary factor"]
- Will the seller consider countering individual offers or is this truly best and final? [TRULY FINAL / MAY COUNTER]

Write a professional email (200-250 words) to the buyer's agents that:
1. Confirms receipt of their offer
2. Informs them of the multiple-offer situation (without disclosing number of offers or terms)
3. Invites their highest and best offer by the deadline
4. Provides guidance on what the seller values (without revealing too much)
5. Is clear about the deadline and how to submit
6. Maintains fairness — same message to all agents

Do NOT disclose the number of offers, offer prices, or any specific terms to any buyer's agent.
Do NOT play agents against each other by hinting at competing offer amounts.
Do NOT guarantee the seller will accept the highest offer — they may consider other factors.

Output format: Ready-to-send email with subject line. One version that goes to all buyer agents (identical).
quickStart: Write a "best and final" request email to send to all buyer agents on my listing at [ADDRESS]. Deadline: [DATE/TIME]. Seller values: [PRIORITIES]. Professional, fair, same message to all. Do not disclose offer details.

title: Commission Objection — "I'll Only Pay X%"
bestFor: ** Listing agents navigating commission conversations in the post-NAR settlement landscape
prompt:
You are a real estate business coach specializing in agent value communication. My seller prospect is pushing back on my commission rate. I need talking points that demonstrate my value without being defensive.

Details:
- My proposed commission/fee structure: [DESCRIBE — e.g., X% listing side, buyer agent compensation offered/not offered, flat fee, etc.]
- Seller's objection: [e.g., "I'll only pay X%," "My friend's agent charged less," "Why should I pay the buyer's agent?," "I can sell it myself on Zillow"]
- My market: [CITY/STATE]
- My value proposition highlights: [e.g., average days on market for my listings, sale-to-list ratio, marketing plan, negotiation track record, number of transactions, specific marketing investments]
- Average agent performance in my market (if known): [e.g., average DOM, average sale-to-list ratio]

Write a conversation framework (400-500 words) that:
1. Acknowledges the objection without being defensive — commission discussions are normal
2. Reframes the conversation from "cost" to "net result"
3. Explains what my fee covers in concrete, tangible terms (not vague "full service" language)
4. Addresses the post-settlement reality: how buyer agent compensation works now and why offering it may benefit the seller
5. Uses a specific math example showing how better negotiation or marketing can more than offset the fee
6. Provides a confident close — not an ultimatum, but not a cave either

Include 2-3 responses to common follow-up objections.

Do NOT disparage discount brokers or FSBO sellers.
Do NOT claim all agents provide the same service.
Do NOT be inflexible — if there's room for creative fee structures, mention them.

Output format: Conversation script with objection-response pairs.
quickStart: My seller prospect objects to my [X%] commission. Write talking points that reframe from cost to value, explain what the fee covers concretely, address buyer agent compensation, and include a math example. Confident, not defensive.

title: Buyer Wants to Back Out After Going Under Contract
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents when a buyer gets cold feet after mutual acceptance
variables: WHICH CONTINGENCIES ARE STILL ACTIVE, IF ANY
prompt:
You are a real estate advisor. My buyer wants to back out of a purchase after going under contract. I need to have an honest conversation about their options, obligations, and consequences.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Contract price: [PRICE]
- Earnest money at stake: [AMOUNT]
- Reason buyer wants to back out: [e.g., cold feet, found another property, financial change, buyer's remorse, family pressure, job uncertainty]
- Current contingency status: [WHICH CONTINGENCIES ARE STILL ACTIVE, IF ANY]
- Days until contingency deadlines: [LIST]
- Has the buyer signed any contingency releases? [YES — which ones / NO]
- Are there legitimate grounds for termination? [e.g., unresolved inspection issue, financing denial, appraisal issue — or purely cold feet]

Write a conversation script (400-500 words) that:
1. Listens first — acknowledge their feelings without judgment
2. Explores the real reason (sometimes the stated reason isn't the real one)
3. Explains the contractual reality clearly:
   - What contingencies are still active and what they protect
   - What happens to earnest money under different scenarios
   - Potential legal exposure if they breach the contract
4. Presents all options:
   - Terminate within an active contingency (if available)
   - Negotiate a mutual release
   - Breach the contract (and consequences)
   - Address the underlying concern and move forward
5. Gives honest counsel — is this a legitimate reason to walk away, or will they regret it?

Do NOT pressure the buyer to stay in a deal they genuinely want out of.
Do NOT provide legal advice about breach of contract — recommend they consult an attorney if it comes to that.
Do NOT guarantee they can get their earnest money back unless a contingency clearly applies.
quickStart: My buyer wants to back out of their contract on [ADDRESS]. Earnest money: [AMOUNT]. Reason: [REASON]. Active contingencies: [LIST]. Write talking points covering their options, contractual obligations, earnest money implications, and my honest counsel.

title: Seller Restricting Showing Access — Professional but Firm Message
bestFor: ** Listing agents whose seller is making it difficult to show the property
prompt:
You are a real estate communication coach. My seller is being unreasonably restrictive about showing access (e.g., only allowing showings at narrow time windows, requiring 48-hour notice, canceling last minute, not allowing weekend showings). This is hurting our ability to sell the home. I need to address it professionally.

Details:
- Current showing restrictions: [DESCRIBE]
- Impact so far: [e.g., X showing requests denied or canceled, buyer agents expressing frustration, feedback that scheduling is too difficult]
- How long on market: [DAYS]
- Number of showings vs. comparable listings: [IF KNOWN]
- Any legitimate reason for restrictions: [e.g., pets, disability, work schedule, tenants — or no clear reason]
- Seller's personality/communication preference: [e.g., direct, sensitive, data-driven, emotional]

Write a message (250-350 words) that:
1. Acknowledges the inconvenience of showings — it IS disruptive to daily life
2. Presents data on showing activity vs. results
3. Explains the direct connection between access and sale price/timeline
4. Proposes a specific, reasonable solution (not "just open up everything")
5. Is firm but respectful — this is advice they need to hear

Do NOT be passive-aggressive about the restrictions.
Do NOT threaten or imply you'll drop the listing.
Do NOT ignore legitimate reasons for limited access — work with them.

Output format: Email or text message (specify: [EMAIL / TEXT]).
quickStart: My seller at [ADDRESS] is restricting showings to [RESTRICTIONS]. We've been on market [X] days with only [Y] showings. Write a firm but respectful message explaining how access affects the sale and proposing a reasonable compromise. Format as [EMAIL / TEXT].

title: Unresponsive Co-Op Agent — Escalation Email
bestFor: ** Any agent dealing with an unresponsive agent on the other side of a transaction
prompt:
You are a real estate communication specialist. The agent on the other side of my transaction has been unresponsive, and it is putting the deal at risk. I need to send a professional escalation email.

Details:
- Transaction property: [ADDRESS]
- The unresponsive agent's role: [LISTING AGENT / BUYER'S AGENT]
- What I've tried so far: [e.g., 3 calls, 2 emails, 1 text over the past X days — no response]
- What I need from them: [e.g., signed addendum, repair response, closing docs, scheduling coordination, contingency deadline response]
- Deadline at risk: [SPECIFIC DATE and what expires/happens]
- Consequences of continued non-response: [e.g., contingency expires, closing delayed, buyer may walk]

Write two emails:
1. **Final attempt to the agent** (200-250 words) — professional, clear about urgency, documents previous attempts, states deadline and consequences. Not angry, but unmistakably serious.
2. **Escalation to their broker** (200-250 words) — to be sent if email #1 gets no response within [TIMEFRAME]. Professional, factual, requests the broker's assistance. Documents the timeline of attempts.

Do NOT be hostile or threatening.
Do NOT copy the broker on the first email (give the agent one more chance).
Do NOT make personal attacks on the agent's competence.

Output format: Two separate emails, each with subject line, ready to send.
quickStart: The [LISTING/BUYER'S] agent on my transaction at [ADDRESS] has been unresponsive for [X] days. I need [SPECIFIC ITEM] by [DEADLINE]. Write a firm final-attempt email to the agent and an escalation email to their broker. Professional, factual, serious.

title: Closing Delay — Email to All Parties
bestFor: ** Any agent managing a closing delay due to lender, title, or other issues
variables: LENDER / TITLE COMPANY / BUYER / SELLER / OTHER
prompt:
You are a real estate transaction communication specialist. We have learned that closing on my transaction may be delayed. I need to notify all relevant parties promptly and professionally.

Details:
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Original closing date: [DATE]
- Reason for delay: [e.g., lender needs additional documentation, title issue discovered, appraisal delay, inspection follow-up, survey issue, HOA docs delayed]
- Expected new closing date: [DATE or "TBD — waiting on [X]"]
- Who caused the delay: [LENDER / TITLE COMPANY / BUYER / SELLER / OTHER]
- Impact on any party: [e.g., seller has movers scheduled, buyer's rate lock expiring, rent-back implications]
- What is being done to resolve it: [ACTIONS BEING TAKEN]
- Who needs to receive this email: [LIST — e.g., other agent, lender, title company, client]

Write a professional email (250-350 words) that:
1. States the situation clearly and immediately (no burying the lead)
2. Explains the reason for the delay without assigning blame
3. Provides the expected resolution timeline
4. Lists what each party needs to do (if anything)
5. Offers reassurance that the deal is still on track (if it is)
6. Invites questions and provides your direct contact

Tone: Calm, transparent, solutions-focused. The recipients will be stressed — your job is to lower the temperature, not raise it.

Do NOT blame any party in an email that goes to everyone.
Do NOT speculate about the reason — state only what you know.
Do NOT minimize the impact — if movers need to be rescheduled, acknowledge it.

Output format: One email suitable for sending to all parties, with subject line. If different messages are needed for different parties, note where to customize.
quickStart: Closing on [ADDRESS] is delayed from [ORIGINAL DATE] to [NEW DATE]. Reason: [REASON]. Write a calm, professional email to all parties (agents, lender, title, clients) explaining the delay, resolution steps, and next actions. No blame.

title: Firing a Difficult Client — The Professional Breakup
bestFor: ** Agents who need to terminate a client relationship professionally
prompt:
You are a real estate business communication coach. I have decided to end my professional relationship with a client, and I need to do it professionally, clearly, and in a way that protects me legally.

Details:
- Client type: [BUYER / SELLER]
- Agreement type: [BUYER AGENCY AGREEMENT / LISTING AGREEMENT / INFORMAL — no signed agreement]
- Reason for termination: [e.g., unreasonable demands, abusive behavior, refusal to follow advice, asking me to act unethically, financial situation changed and they can't perform, they've been working with other agents behind my back]
- Contract termination clause: [DESCRIBE if there's a specific termination provision — or "I need to check"]
- Any active transactions? [YES — describe status / NO]
- Anything owed to you? [e.g., marketing expenses, outstanding invoices]
- Referral offer: [AM I WILLING TO REFER THEM TO ANOTHER AGENT? YES / NO]

Write:
1. **A professional termination letter or email** (250-350 words) that:
   - Is clear and unambiguous — the relationship is ending
   - Does not detail every grievance (keep it high-level)
   - References the agreement and its termination provision
   - Addresses any practical next steps (return of keys, sign removal, MLS status)
   - Offers a referral to another agent (if appropriate)
   - Wishes them well (even if you don't mean it — it's professional)
   - Protects you by documenting the termination in writing

2. **A brief script** for the phone call or in-person conversation that should precede the written notice.

Do NOT list every offense or make it personal.
Do NOT burn the bridge — real estate is a small world.
Do NOT terminate mid-transaction without consulting your broker and potentially an attorney.

Output format: Email/letter with subject line, plus phone conversation talking points (5-7 bullets).
quickStart: I need to end my [BUYER/SELLER] relationship with a client. Reason: [BRIEF REASON]. Agreement type: [TYPE]. Write a professional termination email and phone call talking points. Clear, respectful, protective. No grievance list.

title: Under-Contract Milestone Emails — 5 Templates
bestFor: ** Buyer's and listing agents keeping clients informed throughout the transaction
prompt:
You are a real estate client communication specialist. I need a series of milestone update emails to send to my client at each major stage of the transaction. These should keep them informed, reduce anxiety, and make them feel well-served.

Transaction details:
- My role: [BUYER'S AGENT / LISTING AGENT]
- Client name(s): [NAME]
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Contract price: [PRICE]
- Key dates:
  - Under contract date: [DATE]
  - Inspection deadline: [DATE]
  - Appraisal deadline: [DATE]
  - Financing contingency deadline: [DATE]
  - Closing date: [DATE]

Write 5 email templates:

1. **"We're Under Contract!"** — Celebration + what happens next + immediate action items for the client
2. **"Inspection Complete"** — Summary of where we stand after inspection, next steps, what to expect
3. **"Appraisal Update"** — Appraisal is ordered/completed, what this means, timeline
4. **"Clear to Close"** — We've passed all contingencies, what to prepare for closing day
5. **"Closing Week"** — Final reminders, what to bring, wire instructions warning, what to expect day-of

Each email should be 150-250 words, warm but professional, and include:
- A clear subject line
- 2-3 action items or key dates
- A reassuring close

Do NOT include actual wire instructions in any email (security risk).
Do NOT use so much jargon that a first-time buyer would be lost.
Do NOT make promises about outcomes at any stage.

Output format: 5 separate email templates, clearly labeled by milestone.
quickStart: Write 5 milestone email templates for my [BUYER/SELLER] client on [ADDRESS]. Milestones: under contract, inspection complete, appraisal update, clear to close, closing week. Each 150-250 words, warm, professional, with action items. Include subject lines.

title: Closing Timeline Explainer — First-Time Buyer
bestFor: ** Buyer's agents working with first-time homebuyers
prompt:
You are a real estate educator. My first-time buyer is under contract and feeling overwhelmed by the process ahead. I need a clear, friendly email explaining the closing timeline and what to expect at each stage.

Details:
- Buyer name(s): [NAME]
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Under contract date: [DATE]
- Closing date: [DATE]
- Total days in escrow: [NUMBER]
- Key milestones and dates:
  - Home inspection: [DATE]
  - Appraisal: [DATE ORDERED or EXPECTED]
  - Loan approval deadline: [DATE]
  - Final walkthrough: [DATE or "TBD — usually 24-48 hours before closing"]
  - Closing: [DATE]
- Anything unique about their transaction: [e.g., new construction, FHA requirements, seller rent-back, etc.]

Write an email (400-500 words) that:
1. Congratulates them and normalizes their anxiety ("every first-time buyer feels this way")
2. Provides a numbered or visual timeline from today to closing day
3. Explains each milestone in 1-2 plain-language sentences
4. Lists what THEY need to do vs. what happens behind the scenes
5. Includes a "DO" and "DON'T" list for the escrow period:
   - DO: Keep your job, respond to lender requests quickly, maintain insurance
   - DON'T: Open new credit cards, make large purchases, change bank accounts, co-sign loans
6. Invites questions — "There are no dumb questions"
7. Closes with excitement and reassurance

Do NOT use industry jargon without explaining it.
Do NOT overwhelm them with legal disclosures — keep it practical.
Do NOT skip the financial "don'ts" — they are critical and first-time buyers often don't know them.

Output format: Ready-to-send email with subject line.
quickStart: Write a closing timeline email for my first-time buyer on [ADDRESS]. Contract to close: [START DATE] to [CLOSING DATE]. Include milestone dates, what they need to do at each stage, and the critical financial "don'ts" during escrow. Warm, encouraging, jargon-free.

title: Wire Fraud Warning Email
bestFor: ** All agents — send this to every client approaching closing
prompt:
You are a real estate client protection specialist. I need a clear, direct wire fraud warning email to send to my client before closing. Wire fraud is the biggest financial threat in real estate transactions, and this email could save my client from losing their entire down payment.

Details:
- Client name(s): [NAME]
- Closing date: [DATE]
- Title company name: [NAME]
- Title company phone number (verified): [NUMBER]
- How wire instructions will be delivered: [e.g., "in person at the title office" or "via encrypted email from [SPECIFIC EMAIL ADDRESS]"]

Write a wire fraud warning email (250-350 words) that:
1. Opens with a clear, attention-grabbing statement — this is important, not routine
2. Explains what wire fraud is in plain language (hackers impersonating the title company and sending fake wire instructions)
3. Provides 3-5 specific rules:
   - NEVER wire money based on instructions received via email alone
   - ALWAYS call the title company at a KNOWN number (not one from an email) to verify
   - Be suspicious of any last-minute changes to wire instructions
   - Report any suspicious emails immediately
   - [TITLE COMPANY] will NEVER change wire instructions at the last minute
4. Includes the verified title company phone number to call
5. Tells them what to do if they think they've been targeted
6. Closes with "when in doubt, call me or the title company directly"

Formatting: Use bold text, bullet points, and clear spacing. This email should be scannable, not a wall of text.

Do NOT include actual wire instructions in this email (or any email).
Do NOT downplay the risk — this is a real and growing threat.
Do NOT make it so alarming that the client panics — inform and empower.

Output format: Ready-to-send email with subject line. Subject line should convey urgency without triggering spam filters.
quickStart: Write a wire fraud warning email for my client closing on [DATE] with [TITLE COMPANY]. Include the verified phone number: [NUMBER]. Cover what wire fraud is, 3-5 safety rules, and what to do if targeted. Clear, urgent, not panicky. Include bold formatting for key points.

title: Post-Closing Thank You + What's Next
bestFor: ** All agents — the most important relationship-building email you'll send
variables: FIRST-TIME BUYER / MOVE-UP BUYER / DOWNSIZER / INVESTOR / SELLER WHO ALSO BOUGHT / SELLER
prompt:
You are a real estate relationship-building specialist. My client just closed on their home, and I want to send a thoughtful post-closing email that thanks them, provides helpful next steps, and plants the seed for future referrals — without being pushy about it.

Details:
- Client name(s): [NAME]
- My role: [BUYER'S AGENT / LISTING AGENT]
- Property address: [ADDRESS]
- Transaction type: [FIRST-TIME BUYER / MOVE-UP BUYER / DOWNSIZER / INVESTOR / SELLER WHO ALSO BOUGHT / SELLER]
- Something personal I remember about them: [e.g., they mentioned loving the garden, their kids were excited about the pool, they're relocating for a new job, they're relieved to finally be done with the process]
- Did they have a smooth or bumpy transaction? [SMOOTH / HAD CHALLENGES — briefly describe]
- Do I have a referral program? [YES — describe / NO]

Write an email (300-400 words) that:
1. Opens with genuine congratulations and a personal touch (reference the personal detail)
2. If the transaction was challenging, acknowledges their patience and resilience
3. Provides a helpful "first 30 days" checklist:
   - Change locks
   - Update address (USPS, DMV, subscriptions, etc.)
   - Locate and test smoke detectors, water shutoff, electrical panel, HVAC filters
   - Set up utilities if not already done
